subject: decommissioning checklist for subdomains
truth: Establish a formal decommissioning checklist that includes identifying all DNS records, pointing to a maintenance page, removing DNS, waiting for TTL propagation, decommissioning the resource, revoking certificates, updating inventory, removing from OAuth/CSP/CORS configs, and running a takeover detection scan.
quote: Create a formal checklist that teams must follow when removing any externally facing service
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: JavaScript encoding for JavaScript context
truth: When inserting untrusted data into JavaScript contexts (inside a quoted string, event handler, or script block), use JavaScript encoding that escapes all characters except alphanumeric with \uXXXX unicode escape format and never use untrusted data in JavaScript execution contexts.
quote: - [XSS Experimental Minimal Encoding Rules](https://wiki.owasp.org/index.php/XSS_Experimental_Minimal_Encoding_Rules) Provides examples and guidelines for experimental minimal encoding strategies to p
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: convergent encryption risk
truth: Do not use convergent encryption for secrets without ensuring the system has sufficient resource challenges during encryption and that secrets are of adequate length, otherwise attackers can derive plaintext secrets by generating matching ciphertexts.
quote: it allows attackers to use the system to generate a set of cryptographic strings that might end up in the same secret, allowing the attacker to derive the plaintext secret
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SameSite cookie attribute as sole CSRF defense
truth: Do not rely on SameSite alone for CSRF protection unless the app shares no registrable domain, has no state-changing GET endpoints, uses SameSite=Strict or Lax with __Host- prefix, and has Origin/Referer verification on state-changing endpoints.
quote: `SameSite` is useful as a defense-in-depth control but it does not replace a proper CSRF defense in most deployments.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Legacy systems with limited logging
truth: Stop relying on legacy systems' own logs for security monitoring; deploy network-based detection tools to monitor traffic patterns, connection attempts, and data flows for systems that don't provide detailed security logs.
quote: Older systems often don't provide the detailed security logs you need for modern threat detection and compliance requirements
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: OpenSSL NULL certificate after X509_V_OK
truth: After OpenSSL verification, always call SSL_get_verify_result AND check that SSL_get_peer_certificate returns non-NULL, because a server that does not supply a certificate will result in X509_V_OK with a NULL certificate.
quote: By design, a server that does not supply a certificate will result in `X509_V_OK` with a **NULL** certificate.
source: OWASP CS: Pinning Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Logging sensitive data
truth: Never log sensitive personal data, authentication passwords, session IDs, access tokens, encryption keys, database connection strings, or payment card data directly; instead remove, mask, sanitize, hash, or encrypt them.
quote: Never log data unless it is legally sanctioned
source: OWASP CS: Logging Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Prompt context leakage of secrets
truth: Configure AI tools to exclude sensitive directories from context — AI coding assistants send open files, project structure, and terminal output to the model provider's API, which may contain credentials and secrets.
quote: AI coding assistants send code context (open files, project structure, terminal output) to the model provider's API. This context may contain credentials, personal data, proprietary business logic, and internal architecture details.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: email change with MFA
truth: When the user HAS MFA enabled, use MFA for identity verification instead of the current password, and send a notification-only email to the current address plus a confirmation-required email to the new address.
quote: Request the use of [Multifactor Authentication](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Multifactor_Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html) for identity verification.
source: OWASP CS: Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Client-side CSRF bypasses token and SameSite defenses
truth: Do not assume CSRF tokens or SameSite cookies alone protect against client-side CSRF; the JavaScript program includes tokens in forged requests and browsers send cookies in same-site JS-initiated requests.
quote: **Note:** These variants of CSRF are particularly important as they can bypass some of the common anti-CSRF countermeasures like [token-based mitigations](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheet
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: npm install with lifecycle scripts enabled
truth: Stop running npm install or yarn install without --ignore-scripts; add ignore-scripts=true to .npmrc and use an allowlist like @lavamoat/allow-scripts for packages that legitimately need lifecycle scripts.
quote: - When installing packages make sure to add the `--ignore-scripts` suffix to disable the execution of any scripts by third-party packages.
source: OWASP CS: NPM Security best practices
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Geofencing as MFA factor
truth: Stop relying on geofencing as a standalone MFA factor; it provides no protection if the user's system is compromised, against rogue insiders, or against attackers physically close to the trusted location.
quote: Doesn't provide any protection if the user's system is compromised
source: OWASP CS: Multifactor Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MX records pointing to deprovisioned mail services
truth: Stop leaving MX records pointing to deprovisioned mail services; remove the MX record when the mail service is decommissioned to prevent email interception and certificate-based domain validation attacks.
quote: - **MX records** pointing to deprovisioned mail services can allow an attacker to receive email for the subdomain.
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Behavioral profiling as standalone MFA factor
truth: Stop using behavioral profiling as a standalone MFA factor; early implementations were often inaccurate and caused significant false positives, and it requires large amounts of data and processing power.
quote: Early implementations of behavioral profiling were often inaccurate and caused a significant number of false positives
source: OWASP CS: Multifactor Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store encryption
truth: Stop using AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store without explicitly enabling encryption; do use AWS Secrets Manager which encrypts by default, or explicitly enable encryption on Parameter Store parameters.
quote: you'll need to make sure you've specified encryption yourself (secrets manager does that by default)
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Web service authorization on every request
truth: Authorize clients on every request by checking privileges for the requested method and resource, and add challenge-response authorization to sensitive resources like password changes and contact details.
quote: A web service should authorize its clients whether they have access to the method in question
source: OWASP CS: Web Service Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Predefined safe request data list
truth: Store a predefined list of safe endpoint/method combinations in code and use a switch parameter from the URL to select which entry to use, instead of constructing requests directly from URL fragments.
quote: store a list of predefined, safe request data in the JavaScript code (e.g., combinations of endpoints, request methods and other parameters that are safe to be replayed). The program can then use a switch parameter in the URL fragment to decide which entry of the list should each JavaScript function use
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: callback idempotency
truth: Implement idempotency: only process an order once regardless of how many times the callback is received, and use a unique transaction ID with an expiry timestamp to mitigate callback replay attacks.
quote: Implement idempotency: only process an order once regardless of how many times the callback is received
source: OWASP CS: Secure Integration of Third-Party Payment Gateways Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XSS via HTTP Parameter Pollution in server-side templates
truth: Stop trusting URL parameters like content_type without encoding or validation; always validate that numeric parameters are actually integers and encode all user input before embedding in JavaScript.
quote: As a result, in this example the main flaw is trusting the content_type in the `Share` page without proper encoding or validation.
source: OWASP CS: XSS Filter Evasion Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: iOS Siri intents exposing sensitive functionality without authentication
truth: Stop leaving Siri intents accessible without authentication; set requiresUserAuthentication to true and userConfirmationRequired to true on intents exposing sensitive information or functionality.
quote: Configure `requiresUserAuthentication` to `true` on intents that expose
source: OWASP CS: Mobile Application Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Rules files as persistent agent steering
truth: Treat .cursorrules, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and .github/copilot-instructions.md as security-sensitive configuration that can embed persistent instructions; review changes to these files in every PR.
quote: - **Rules files as persistent steering.** Files like `.cursorrules`, `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `.github/copilot-instructions.md`, and `.windsurfrules` silently steer every future generation.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: logging resource exhaustion events
truth: Avoid logging full prompts or tool inputs/outputs when detecting resource exhaustion; log only measured usage (tokens in/out), policy thresholds, request identifiers, and the tool/model involved.
quote: Avoid logging full prompts or tool inputs/outputs. Prefer logging measured usage (tokens in/out), policy thresholds, request identifiers, and the tool/model involved.
source: OWASP CS: Application Logging Vocabulary Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Storing authentication tokens in localStorage/sessionStorage
truth: Do not store authentication tokens, session IDs, JWTs, refresh tokens, or any credential in localStorage or sessionStorage; use HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict cookies or a BFF pattern instead.
quote: > Do not store authentication tokens, session IDs, JWTs, refresh tokens, or any credential in `localStorage` or `sessionStorage`.
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

associative: store auth token in web storage

subject: detecting session ID anomalies
truth: Implement built-in intrusion detection detection points for session anomalies such as cookie modification, new cookie added, session ID reuse from another user, or User-Agent changes mid-session.
quote: establish multiple session related detection points, such as when an existing cookie is modified or deleted, a new cookie is added, the session ID from another user is reused, or when the user location or User-Agent changes
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XSS output encoding via HTTP interceptor/filter
truth: Do not rely on HTTP interceptors or servlet filters for XSS output encoding; perform output encoding as close to where data is rendered as possible, with full knowledge of the rendering context.
quote: perform output encoding as close to where the data is rendered is possible
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Legacy systems on flat trusted networks
truth: Stop assuming legacy systems can have direct network access; isolate them in separate monitored network zones with restricted access and require modern authentication for any access to the zone.
quote: Older systems were designed for flat, trusted networks where everything inside the perimeter was considered safe
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: OAuth 2.0 Access Token Privilege Restriction
truth: Restrict access tokens to the minimum required privileges, including audience restriction to a single Resource Server and resource/action scoping via scope and authorization_details parameters.
quote: The privileges associated with an access token should be restricted to the minimum required for the particular application or use case
source: OWASP CS: OAuth 2.0 Protocol Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AI-suggested package installation
truth: Stop blindly running npm install, pip install, or go get with AI-suggested package names without verification; verify every AI-suggested package exists on the public registry before installing.
quote: - Blindly run `npm install`, `pip install`, or `go get` with package names suggested by an AI without verification.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AI test fabrication and deletion
truth: Require human review of all AI-generated test modifications — AI agents make CI green by deleting failing tests, weakening assertions, or mocking the unit under test instead of fixing the code.
quote: AI agents make CI green by deleting failing tests, weakening assertions, mocking the unit under test instead of fixing the code, or asserting the buggy behaviour.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AWS CloudFront distribution deletion with dangling CNAME
truth: Delete the CloudFront distribution AND remove the CNAME record pointing to *.cloudfront.net; otherwise an attacker can create a new distribution and add your domain as an alternate domain name.
quote: | AWS CloudFront | Distribution deleted while CNAME remains | When a CloudFront distribution is deleted but the CNAME record still points to the `*.cloudfront.net` hostname, an attacker can create a n
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SSRF proof-of-legitimacy token
truth: For requests to external IPs/domains, require the target application to generate a random 20-character alphanumeric token passed via a parameter name matching [a-z]{1,10} in an HTTP POST body.
quote: alphanumeric of 20 characters) that is expected to be passed by the caller (in body via a parameter for which the name is also defined by the application itself and only allow characters set `[a-z]{1,
source: OWASP CS: Server-Side Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CAN bus communication
truth: Stop sending messages on the CAN bus without authentication or encryption; implement message authentication codes (MACs) and use secure CAN (CAN-FD with authentication) or a gateway firewall.
quote: Many vehicles use communication protocols like CAN (Controller Area Network) without adequate security measures
source: OWASP CS: Top 10 Automotive Security Vulnerabilities
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: iOS Shortcuts executing sensitive actions while device is locked
truth: Stop allowing Shortcuts to execute sensitive app functions without device unlock; validate secure tokens from Keychain and check isProtectedDataAvailable before executing sensitive shortcuts.
quote: Sensitive app functionalities triggered via Shortcuts should always require device unlock before execution.
source: OWASP CS: Mobile Application Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: RAG document ingestion from untrusted sources
truth: Scan ingested documents for adversarial patterns (prompt injection markers, hidden instructions, invisible Unicode characters, zero-width spaces) and maintain an allowlist of trusted sources.
quote: Scan ingested documents for known adversarial patterns (prompt injection markers, hidden instructions, invisible Unicode characters, zero-width spaces).
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XMLReader has external entity processing enabled by default
truth: Disable doctype declaration and external entity features on XMLReader by setting disallow-doctype-decl, load-external-dtd, external-general-entities, and external-parameter-entities features.
quote: To protect the Java `org.xml.sax.XMLReader` from an XXE attack, do this:
source: OWASP CS: XML External Entity Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: semantic input validation
truth: Apply semantic validation to enforce correctness of values in the specific business context (e.g., start date before end date, price within expected range), not just syntactic format checks.
quote: - **Semantic** validation should enforce correctness of their *values* in the specific business context (e.g.
source: OWASP CS: Input Validation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: flat network deployment
truth: Stop deploying services on a single flat network; segment FRONTEND, MIDDLEWARE, and BACKEND behind separate firewalls so that compromising a web server does not grant direct database access.
quote: executing arbitrary commands on a public web server (NginX, Apache, Internet Information Service) prevents an attacker from gaining direct access to the database
source: OWASP CS: Network segmentation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AI coding agent processing untrusted repository content
truth: Treat all repository content (issues, PRs, comments, READMEs) as untrusted input when processed by an AI coding agent; do not assume content is safe because it appears in a familiar context.
quote: Treat all repository content (issues, PRs, comments, READMEs) as untrusted input when processed by an AI coding agent.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AI rules files as attack surface
truth: Treat AI rules files (.cursorrules, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, copilot-instructions.md) as security-critical configuration — an attacker who modifies them controls every subsequent AI generation.
quote: These files are a persistence mechanism -- an attacker who can modify them controls every subsequent generation.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: JWT HMAC secret generation
truth: Generate HMAC secrets using secrets.token_bytes() with at least 256 bits for HS256 and 512 bits for HS512; never use passwords, hardcoded values, random.randbytes, or insufficient entropy.
quote: Using a password/passphrase is not OK
source: OWASP CS: JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: simultaneous session logons
truth: If simultaneous session logons are not allowed, implicitly terminate the previously available session after each new authentication event, or ask the user which session must remain active.
quote: If the web application does not want to allow simultaneous session logons, it must take effective actions after each new authentication event, implicitly terminating the previously available session
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Storing secrets in Web Workers
truth: For secrets that frontend JavaScript must access, store them in a Web Worker and never transmit the secret to the main window context; this provides HttpOnly-cookie-level confidentiality.
quote: Storing secrets within the memory of a Web Worker offers the same security guarantees as an HttpOnly cookie
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Password length and composition rules
truth: Enforce a minimum password length (8 chars with MFA, 15 without) and a maximum of at least 64 characters; allow all characters including unicode and whitespace with no composition rules.
quote: There should be no password composition rules limiting the type of characters permitted.
source: OWASP CS: Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Prototype-based data skimming in extension main-world scripts
truth: Stop executing scripts with sensitive user information in the web page's main-world context where prototypes can be polluted; use only non-sensitive data for cross-context communication.
quote: When an extension uses sensitive user information in any scripts executed on the web page's context, the data becomes accessible to the page's scripts.
source: OWASP CS: Browser Extension Security Vulnerabilities Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Client-side CSRF input validation gap
truth: Validate that async request endpoints and methods derived from attacker-controllable inputs only allow non-state-changing operations (e.g., GET only, endpoints with a predefined prefix).
quote: input validation checks has to be implemented. These checks should strictly assess the format and choice of the values of the request parameters and decide whether they can only be used in non-state-changing operations
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Azure App Service deletion
truth: Delete the Azure App Service AND remove the DNS CNAME record pointing to *.azurewebsites.net; otherwise any Azure tenant can create a new app with the same name and claim your subdomain.
quote: App names are globally unique. After deletion, any Azure tenant can create a new app with the same name.
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: runner segregation
truth: Use runner groups and labels to separate high-privilege runners from low-privilege runners, giving granular control over which repositories and workflows can access sensitive resources.
quote: Use [runner groups](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/concepts/runners/runner-groups) and [labels](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/manage-runners/self-hosted-runners/apply-labels) to separ
source: OWASP CS: GitHub Actions Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Microservices security documentation
truth: Stop skipping structured security documentation of microservice building blocks; collect and document service names, API definitions, data storages, message queues, and their relations.
quote: Collect information on the building blocks
source: OWASP CS: Microservices based Security Arch Doc Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MFA factor independence
truth: Stop requiring multiple instances of the same factor (e.g., password + PIN); require factors from different categories that are independent and cannot be compromised by the same attack.
quote: It should be noted that requiring multiple instances of the same authentication factor (such as needing both a password and a PIN) **does not constitute MFA** and offers minimal additional security.
source: OWASP CS: Multifactor Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SameSite=Lax allowing state-changing GET requests
truth: Stop using GET endpoints for state-changing operations when relying on SameSite=Lax; the default Lax behavior still allows cookies on top-level navigations using safe methods like GET.
quote: If any state-changing operation in the application is reachable via a `GET` request, `SameSite=Lax` will not stop it.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CI/CD poisoned pipeline execution (PPE)
truth: Do not allow untrusted code or user-controlled input to define pipeline execution logic; implement strict pipeline-based access controls (PBAC) to prevent poisoned pipeline execution.
quote: Poisoned Pipeline Execution (PPE)
source: OWASP CS: CI/CD Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SameSite cookie attribute set to domain-specific cookie
truth: Do NOT set a cookie specifically for a domain, as all subdomains will share the cookie and a subdomain with a CNAME to domains not in your control introduces a security vulnerability.
quote: be careful to NOT set a cookie specifically for a domain. This action introduces a security vulnerability because all subdomains of that domain will share the cookie, and this is particularly an issue if a subdomain has a CNAME to domains not in your control.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CNAME records pointing to third-party services
truth: Stop creating CNAME records to third-party services without a process to remove them when the service is decommissioned; implement automated monitoring to detect dangling DNS targets.
quote: - **CNAME records** are the most common vector.
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: NS records delegating to third-party DNS providers
truth: Stop leaving NS records that delegate subdomains to third-party DNS providers after closing the provider account; remove the NS delegation before deactivating the DNS service account.
quote: - **NS records** delegating a subdomain to a third-party DNS provider are particularly dangerous.
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: virtual patching
truth: Use a positive security model (allowlist) that validates parameter values against an expected pattern rather than a negative security model (denylist) that blocks specific characters.
quote: Positive security rules, only the other hand, provides better protection
source: OWASP CS: Virtual Patching Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: GitHub Actions workflow static analysis
truth: Enable CodeQL with 'language: actions' and use Zizmor to scan GitHub Actions workflows; configure them to run on every pull request and block merges on high/critical severity issues.
quote: - If available, enable [CodeQL](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/reference/code-scanning/codeql/codeql-queries/actions-built-in-queries) `actions` scanning in your repositories to detect vulne
source: OWASP CS: GitHub Actions Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: baseline vs diff-based review
truth: Perform baseline reviews for new applications, major releases, legacy onboarding, compliance, and post-incident analysis; use diff-based reviews for pull requests and daily changes.
quote: **Baseline Reviews** examine the entire codebase comprehensively.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Code Review Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: positive security model virtual patching
truth: Use a positive security model (allowlist) that specifies characteristics of valid input and denies anything that does not conform, rather than trying to block known attack patterns.
quote: Positive security model (allowlist) is a comprehensive security mechanism that provides an independent input validation envelope to an application.
source: OWASP CS: Virtual Patching Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AI coding agent running without sandboxing
truth: Run AI coding agents in sandboxed environments (dev containers, restricted shells, VMs, ephemeral workspaces); do not run agents with full developer credentials without sandboxing.
quote: Run AI coding agents in sandboxed environments: dev containers, restricted shells, virtual machines, or ephemeral cloud workspaces.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: OS command input validation with allow-list regex
truth: When OS commands must accept user-supplied arguments, validate them against an allow-list regular expression that excludes metacharacters like & | ; $ > < \ ` ! and limits length.
quote: - Allow-list Regular Expression - where is explicitly defined a list of good characters allowed and the maximum length of the string.
source: OWASP CS: Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: iOS deep links bypassing authentication
truth: Stop allowing deep links to bypass authentication; implement authentication checks on view controllers accessed via deep links and redirect unauthorized users to the login screen.
quote: Deep links offer direct access to specific app screens, which could potentially bypass authentication if not secured
source: OWASP CS: Mobile Application Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: domain verification for cloud services
truth: Retain domain verification TXT records (e.g., Azure asuid, Google Search Console) even after decommissioning the cloud resource to prevent another tenant from claiming the domain.
quote: Keep this TXT record in place even after decommissioning the App Service to prevent another tenant from claiming the domain.
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XML Schema numeric type for quantity/price
truth: Use positiveInteger instead of integer for quantity to prevent negative-value logical attacks, and use decimal instead of float/double for price to prevent Infinity/NaN injection.
quote: this data type may allow negative values, it might allow a negative result on the user's account if an attacker provides a negative number. What you probably want to see in here to avoid that logical vulnerability is positiveInteger instead of integer.
source: OWASP CS: XML Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: email change without MFA
truth: When the user does NOT have MFA enabled, require the current password for identity verification AND send confirmation-required emails to BOTH the current and new email addresses.
quote: Request the user's current password for identity verification.
source: OWASP CS: Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: encryption algorithm selection for secrets
truth: Select an algorithm that provides both encryption and authentication simultaneously, such as AES-256-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305, rather than using unauthenticated encryption modes.
quote: Please note that in all cases, we need to preferably select an algorithm that provides encryption and confidentiality at the same time, such as AES-256 using GCM [(Galois Counter Mode)](https://en.wik
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: binding session ID to IP address for security
truth: Do not rely on client IP address binding to defend against session hijacking; a skilled attacker can bypass this by sharing the same network or using the same outbound web proxy.
quote: a skilled attacker can bypass these controls by reusing the same IP address assigned to the victim user by sharing the same network
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: server-side cart validation
truth: Validate all cart details (product IDs, prices, discounts) on the backend and recalculate totals server-side using trusted data before order creation to prevent price tampering.
quote: Validate all cart details (product IDs, prices, discounts) on the backend
source: OWASP CS: Secure Integration of Third-Party Payment Gateways Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XSS via event handlers in HTML tags
truth: Stop allowing user input to be placed into HTML event handler attributes without proper output encoding; use context-sensitive encoding or a CSP that disables inline JavaScript.
quote: This attack, which uses normal XSS JavaScript injection, serves as a baseline
source: OWASP CS: XSS Filter Evasion Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Origin/Referer header verification for CSRF
truth: Verify the Origin header matches the target origin; if absent, verify the Referer header hostname matches the target origin, and block requests where neither header is present.
quote: If the Origin header is not present, verify that the hostname in the Referer header matches the target origin.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: File upload storage location
truth: Store uploaded files on a different server or outside the webroot; if public access is needed, use a handler that maps an internal ID to the file instead of direct file access.
quote: Store the files on a different server. If that's not possible, store them outside of the webroot
source: OWASP CS: File Upload Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: input validation as primary defense
truth: Do not use input validation as the primary method of preventing XSS, SQL Injection, or other injection attacks; use dedicated output encoding and parameterized queries instead.
quote: Input Validation should not be used as the *primary* method of preventing [XSS](Cross_Site_Scripting_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.md), [SQL Injection](SQL_Injection_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.md) and other atta
source: OWASP CS: Input Validation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Node.js X-XSS-Protection header default
truth: Set X-XSS-Protection to 0 to disable the XSS Auditor instead of enabling it, as modern browsers deprecated the header and enabling it can introduce client-side security issues.
quote: its use can introduce additional security issues on the client side
source: OWASP CS: NodeJS Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy (COOP) for frame counting
truth: Set the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header to 'same-origin' or 'same-origin-allow-popups' to prevent cross-origin documents from accessing the window object and counting frames.
quote: Setting this header will prevent cross-origin documents from opening in the same browsing context group.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-site leaks Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Legacy systems with weak authentication
truth: Stop relying on basic username/password authentication for legacy systems; deploy an identity-aware proxy that handles MFA and session management in front of the legacy system.
quote: Many older systems only support basic username/password authentication with no option for multi-factor authentication
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: storing encryption keys in the same location as encrypted data
truth: Store encryption keys in a separate location from encrypted data (e.g., keys in filesystem, data in database) so an attacker cannot access both through a single vulnerability.
quote: encryption keys should be stored in a separate location from encrypted data
source: OWASP CS: Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: PKCE downgrade attack mitigation
truth: Authorization servers must ensure a token request containing a code_verifier parameter is accepted only if a code_challenge parameter was present in the authorization request.
quote: Authorization Servers are mitigating PKCE Downgrade Attacks by ensuring a token request containing a "code_verifier" parameter is accepted only if a "code_challenge" parameter is present in the authorization request.
source: OWASP CS: OAuth 2.0 Protocol Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: encrypting sensitive data at the wrong layer
truth: Choose the encryption layer (application, database, filesystem, or hardware) based on your threat model; hardware-level encryption does not protect against remote compromise.
quote: hardware level encryption is effective at protecting against the physical theft of the server, but will provide no protection if an attacker is able to compromise the server remotely.
source: OWASP CS: Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: GitHub Actions GITHUB_TOKEN permissions
truth: Restrict default GITHUB_TOKEN permissions to 'Read repository contents and packages permissions' and explicitly grant additional permissions in the workflow file if required.
quote: - Restrict default `GITHUB_TOKEN` permissions to `Read repository contents and packages permissions` in the repository settings.
source: OWASP CS: GitHub Actions Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Cloud provider API quotas and self-DoS
truth: Stop ignoring cloud provider API rate limits on secret management services; do spread workloads across accounts/projects and use data key caching to avoid hitting API quotas.
quote: You could potentially (D)DoS yourself when you run into these limits.
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Session ID binding to authentication
truth: Stop treating session IDs as equivalent to authentication without proper binding; ensure the session ID is bound to the user's authentication credentials and access controls.
quote: the session ID (or token) is temporarily equivalent to the strongest authentication method used by the application
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: transaction authorization methods
truth: Implement transaction authorization using a dedicated second factor such as TOTP, SMS OTP, digital signature, or challenge-response token, not by reusing the primary session.
quote: - A time-based one-time password (OTP) token, such as an [OATH TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time-based_One-time_Password_Algorithm)
source: OWASP CS: Transaction Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM prompt structure — instructions vs data separation
truth: Do NOT concatenate user input directly into a prompt; use a structured format that clearly separates SYSTEM_INSTRUCTIONS from USER_DATA_TO_PROCESS with explicit demarcation.
quote: Use structured formats that clearly separate instructions from user data.
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: OAuth 2.0 Implicit Grant
truth: Stop using the Implicit Grant (response_type=token); use Authorization Code Grant with PKCE (response_type=code) for all client types including SPAs and native applications.
quote: Implicit Grant (DEPRECATED — DO NOT USE)
source: OWASP CS: OAuth 2.0 Protocol Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Fetch Metadata CSRF protection without fallback
truth: Always implement a fallback to standard origin verification headers when using Fetch Metadata for CSRF protection, because legacy browsers do not send Sec-Fetch-* headers.
quote: Because some legacy browsers do not send `Sec-Fetch-*` headers, a fallback to [standard origin verification](#using-standard-headers-to-verify-origin) headers **is a mandatory requirement** for any Fe
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Browser caching of PII and credentials in input fields
truth: Stop letting browsers cache PII and credentials in input fields; add spellcheck='false' autocomplete='off' autocorrect='off' autocapitalize='off' to every sensitive input.
quote: tell the input fields not to assist in any way
source: OWASP CS: HTML5 Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: wildcard DNS records
truth: Avoid wildcard DNS records; if required, scope them narrowly (e.g., *.staging.example.com) and combine with a reverse proxy that maintains an allowlist of valid hostnames.
quote: Wildcard DNS records (`*.example.com`) are especially dangerous because they resolve for any subdomain, including those matching services that no longer exist.
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CSP meta tag limitations
truth: When using CSP via a meta tag, you lose framing protections (frame-ancestors), sandboxing, and CSP violation logging endpoints; use the HTTP header instead when possible.
quote: However, you will not be able to use [framing protections](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Security-Policy/frame-ancestors), [sandboxing](https://developer.mozilla.or
source: OWASP CS: Content Security Policy Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: dangerous contexts for variables
truth: Stop placing variables into dangerous contexts (inside script tags, HTML comments, CSS directly, attribute names, tag names) — output encoding will NOT prevent XSS there.
quote: Don't place variables into dangerous contexts as even with output encoding, it will not prevent an XSS attack fully.
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: secrets detection lifecycle
truth: Treat secrets like authorization tokens: rotate them often, automate rotation, enforce least privilege, make them revocable with logging, and never log them in plaintext.
quote: Never be logged
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XSS via javascript: in href/src attributes
truth: Stop allowing user-controlled data to populate href/src attributes with javascript: URIs; validate and sanitize URLs against a whitelist of allowed schemes (http, https).
quote: This attack will bypass most SRC domain filters
source: OWASP CS: XSS Filter Evasion Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Dynamic SQL in stored procedures without bind variables
truth: Stop constructing SQL strings by concatenation inside stored procedures; use bind variables with EXECUTE IMMEDIATE ... USING or sp_executesql with parameter definitions.
quote: Bind variables are used to tell the database that the inputs to this dynamic SQL are 'data' and not possibly code
source: OWASP CS: Query Parameterization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: signed/unsigned comparison
truth: Stop comparing signed integers against unsigned integers without understanding C/C++ promotion rules; enable -Wsign-compare or -Wconversion to catch the -1 > 1 pitfall.
quote: As a concrete example, (and for those not familiar with C/C++ promotion rules), a warning will be issued if a signed integer is promoted to an unsigned integer and then compared because a side effect
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CI/CD artifact integrity validation
truth: Do not deploy artifacts without verifying their integrity; implement cryptographic signing and integrity validation for all build artifacts moving through the pipeline.
quote: Improper Artifact Integrity Validation
source: OWASP CS: CI/CD Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Incomplete tenant data deletion on offboarding
truth: Stop leaving residual tenant data after deletion; implement verified data purging across all storage layers (database, cache, file storage, backups) during offboarding.
quote: Incomplete provisioning or data retention after deletion
source: OWASP CS: Multi-Tenant Application Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: missing egress restrictions on DMZ
truth: DMZ Outgoing segments are defined to have (permitted) outbound access to external networks — do not restrict that. Instead, place DMZ Outgoing servers behind a firewall configured with no rules allowing inbound traffic FROM external/Internet networks INTO that segment, so external hosts cannot initiate connections into it, while the segment's own outbound calls remain allowed.
quote: - _DMZ Outgoing_ - a segment for hosting services that are inaccessible from the Internet, but have access to external networks (the firewall does not contain any rules for allowing traffic from exter
source: OWASP CS: Network segmentation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA
_audit_fix: MISLEADING: The truth statement inverts the source's meaning: the OWASP Network Segmentation Cheat Sheet's 'DMZ Outgoing' segment is explicitly defined to host services tha

subject: secrets management developer onboarding
truth: Provide clear documentation, SDKs, CLI tools, and self-service workflows so developers adopt the secrets management solution instead of resorting to insecure practices.
quote: If the process is too complex, developers may resort to insecure practices
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: TLS cipher suites
truth: Disable null ciphers, anonymous ciphers, EXPORT ciphers, RSA transport, and non-forward-secrecy Diffie-Hellman ciphers; use AEAD ciphers (AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305).
quote: - RSA transport (`TLS_RSA_*`) and ephemeral/static Diffie-Hellman key agreement (`TLS_DH_*`, `TLS_ECDH_*`) which do not provide forward secrecy.
source: OWASP CS: Transport Layer Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: API authentication
truth: Stop relying on network perimeter security for APIs; require authentication and authorization for every API call with request schema validation and behavioral analysis.
quote: requires authentication and authorization for every API call, validates request schemas, and uses behavioral analysis to detect abuse patterns
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM prompt injection input filtering
truth: Stop using simple anagram-based similarity checks; use established string metric libraries (Levenshtein, Damerau-Levenshtein, Jaro-Winkler) for typoglycemia detection.
quote: The `_is_similar_word` helper above is intentionally minimal and only catches anagram-style scrambles.
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Auto-accept mode on AI coding agents
truth: Do not enable auto-accept mode on untrusted or unfamiliar codebases; understand the risk of flags like --dangerously-skip-permissions that bypass confirmation prompts.
quote: Understand and evaluate the risk of flags like `--dangerously-skip-permissions` or auto-accept modes. These bypass confirmation prompts and give the agent unrestricted execution.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: WebSocket authentication
truth: Do not rely on WebSocket's lack of built-in authentication; implement explicit authentication (e.g., tokens in the initial handshake) before allowing message exchange.
quote: No built-in authentication makes access control easy to forget
source: OWASP CS: WebSocket Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM prompt concatenation
truth: Stop directly concatenating user input with system prompts; use input separation, delimiters, or structured API calls that treat user input as data, not instructions.
quote: Vulnerable: Direct concatenation without separation
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Unicode injection via bidi and zero-width characters in code
truth: Detect and flag bidi override characters (U+202A–U+202E, U+2066–U+2069) and zero-width characters (U+200B, U+200C, U+200D, U+FEFF) in code, commits, and agent output.
quote: - Detect and flag bidi override characters (U+202A through U+202E, U+2066 through U+2069) and zero-width characters (U+200B, U+200C, U+200D, U+FEFF) in code, commits, and agent output.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: DNS decommissioning order
truth: Do NOT delete the cloud resource before removing the DNS record; redirect or serve a maintenance page first, then update DNS, wait for propagation, then decommission.
quote: The common mistake is doing these steps in reverse: deleting the cloud resource first, which creates an immediate window for takeover
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Client-Side CSRF via URL hash fragment
truth: Stop using attacker-controllable URL fragments to construct async request endpoints and methods; validate inputs against a predefined allowlist of safe request data.
quote: the JavaScript program uses URL fragments to obtain the server-side endpoint for the asynchronous HTTP request
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: HTML encoding lost on DOM value retrieval
truth: Do not rely on server-side HTML encoding alone when values are retrieved via DOM value attributes; the encoding is reversed on retrieval and code becomes executable.
quote: encodings are lost when you retrieve them using the value attribute of a DOM element
source: OWASP CS: DOM based XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: trusting backend feeds without validation
truth: Validate data from all potentially untrusted sources including backend feeds from suppliers, partners, vendors, and regulators, not just Internet-facing web clients.
quote: Data from all potentially untrusted sources should be subject to input validation, including not only Internet-facing web clients but also backend feeds
source: OWASP CS: Input Validation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MCP server tool descriptions
truth: Stop embedding natural-language instructions or commands in tool descriptions; the LLM treats all description text as behavioral directives, enabling tool poisoning.
quote: Malicious instructions hidden in tool descriptions
source: OWASP CS: MCP (Model Context Protocol) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR) across tenants
truth: Stop trusting user-supplied resource IDs to enforce tenant boundaries; always verify that the authenticated tenant owns the requested resource before returning data.
quote: Accessing resources by manipulating tenant/resource IDs
source: OWASP CS: Multi-Tenant Application Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: weak key derivation in encryption library README
truth: Stop copying README examples that derive AES keys from passphrases using MD5 with a single iteration; use the library's PBKDF2 module with stronger defaults instead.
quote: A widely-used encryption library's README examples derive AES keys from passphrases using MD5 with a single iteration
source: OWASP CS: NPM Security best practices
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: reauthentication after risk events
truth: Require reauthentication (primary credentials or MFA) when high-risk events are detected such as password changes, new device logins, or account recovery completion.
quote: applications should require reauthentication when specific high-risk events are detected
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: unauthenticated access to public resources
truth: Do not require authentication for public resources; authorization is separate from authentication and unauthenticated users may be authorized for certain resources.
quote: authentication is not always required for accessing resources; an unauthenticated user may be authorized to access certain public resources, such as an image or login page
source: OWASP CS: Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Using user-supplied filenames instead of generating random filenames
truth: Generate a random string (UUID/GUID) as the filename instead of using the user-supplied filename to prevent path traversal, special file overwrite, and XSS attacks.
quote: In order to avoid the above mentioned threat, creating a **random string** as a filename, such as generating a UUID/GUID, is essential.
source: OWASP CS: File Upload Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: JWT token storage — localStorage vs sessionStorage
truth: Do not store JWTs/session tokens in localStorage or sessionStorage — both are plain JavaScript-readable Web Storage APIs, so any successful XSS can read either equally; sessionStorage's tab-scoping only limits cross-tab reflected-XSS reach and offers no protection against stored XSS in the same page. Prefer HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookies (or a Backend-for-Frontend pattern) instead. If client-side JS access to the token is unavoidable, hold it only in an in-memory variable/closure (never Web Storage), accepting it will be lost on refresh/restart.
quote: Store the token using the browser *sessionStorage* container, or use JavaScript *closures* with *private* variables
source: OWASP CS: JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA
_audit_fix: MISLEADING: sessionStorage is exactly as accessible to any XSS payload as localStorage (both are plain JS-readable Web Storage APIs), so recommending it 'to limit exposure

subject: AI-suggested dependency versions with CVEs
truth: Stop accepting AI-suggested dependency versions without checking for known CVEs; run dependency auditing tools on every AI-generated dependency list before merging.
quote: Accept AI-suggested dependency versions without checking for known CVEs.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: virtual patching
truth: Do NOT create exploit-specific virtual patches that block only the exact known payload; instead create positive-security rules that validate allowed input patterns.
quote: You want to resist the urge to take the easy road and quickly create an **exploit-specific virtual patch**.
code_bad:
SecRule REQUEST_URI "@contains /wp-content/plugins/levelfourstorefront/scripts/administration/exportsubscribers.php" "chain,id:1,phase:2,t:none,t:Utf8toUnicode,t:urlDecodeUni,t:normalizePathWin,t:lowercase,block,msg:'Input Validation Error for \'reqID\' parameter.',logdata:'%{args.reqid}'"
  SecRule ARGS:/reqID/ "@pm '"
code_good:
SecRule REQUEST_URI "@contains /wp-content/plugins/levelfourstorefront/scripts/administration/exportsubscribers.php" "chain,id:2,phase:2,t:none,t:Utf8toUnicode,t:urlDecodeUni,t:normalizePathWin,t:lowercase,block,msg:'Input Validation Error for \'reqID\' parameter.',logdata:'%{args.reqid}'"
  SecRule ARGS:/reqID/ "!@rx ^[0-9]+$"
source: OWASP CS: Virtual Patching Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: telematics API security
truth: Stop exposing telematics APIs without strong authentication and authorization; implement OAuth 2.0, API keys with least privilege, and encrypt all data in transit.
quote: Telematics units that connect vehicles to cloud services may have insufficient security controls
source: OWASP CS: Top 10 Automotive Security Vulnerabilities
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Human-in-the-loop for high-risk LLM operations
truth: Implement human oversight (HITL) for inputs containing high-risk keywords like password, api_key, admin, system, bypass, override combined with injection patterns.
quote: Implement human oversight for high-risk operations.
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Laravel missing security headers
truth: Add X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, and Content-Security-Policy headers to your Laravel middleware or web server configuration.
quote: You should consider adding the following security headers to your web server or Laravel application middleware
source: OWASP CS: Laravel Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: custom authorization solution in microservices
truth: Base the authorization solution on widely-used solutions instead of building a custom one to avoid maintenance burden, missing SDKs, and lack of community support.
quote: The authorization solution should be based on widely-used solutions because implementing a custom solution has the following cons
source: OWASP CS: Microservices Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SAML Destination attribute validation
truth: Validate the Destination attribute on samlp:Response exactly matches the SP's expected ACS URL and reject responses missing Destination or where it does not match.
quote: Validate the `Destination` attribute on `<samlp:Response>` exactly matches the SP's expected Assertion Consumer Service (ACS) URL
source: OWASP CS: SAML Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: callback trust model
truth: Only server-to-server callbacks should be trusted for payment verification and order fulfillment; user redirection parameters are untrusted and can be manipulated.
quote: Only server-to-server callbacks should be trusted for payment verification and order fulfillment
source: OWASP CS: Secure Integration of Third-Party Payment Gateways Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Deeply nested XML without closing tags for DoS
truth: Stop accepting deeply nested XML elements without closing tags; enforce limits on nesting depth and require well-formed closing tags to prevent stack-overflow DoS.
quote: One popular coercive attack in XML involves parsing deeply nested XML documents without their corresponding ending tags. The idea is to make the victim use up -and eventually deplete- the machine's resources and cause a denial of service on the target.
source: OWASP CS: XML Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: dynamic policy for access decisions
truth: Stop using static rules or permanent permissions; evaluate multiple risk factors (identity, device, location, time, behavior) dynamically for every access request.
quote: Access decisions consider multiple factors
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Kubernetes vulnerability prioritization
truth: Prioritize remediation of vulnerabilities by combining severity score with deployment context (privileged containers, internet exposure, environment criticality).
quote: For example, a deployment containing a vulnerability with severity score of 7 or greater should be moved up in remediation priority if that deployment contains privileged containers and is open to the
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: logging prompt injection events
truth: Avoid logging the full prompt/tool I/O when detecting prompt injection; log only a detection category or rule ID, the target tool/server, and request identifiers.
quote: Avoid logging the full prompt/tool I/O. Prefer logging a detection category or rule ID, the target tool/server, and request identifiers
source: OWASP CS: Application Logging Vocabulary Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: typosquatting npm packages
truth: Do not install packages with names that visually resemble popular packages; verify package authenticity through download counts, GitHub repo, and release history.
quote: bad actors publish malicious modules to the npm registry with names that look much like existing popular modules
source: OWASP CS: NPM Security best practices
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: iOS CA public key pinning via Info.plist
truth: On iOS, pin a CA public key by specifying it in Info.plist under App Transport Security Settings, or use TrustKit, rather than implementing custom SSL validation.
quote: Apple suggests pinning a CA public key by specifying it in `Info.plist` file under [App Transport Security Settings](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/security/preventing_insecure_network_conn
source: OWASP CS: Pinning Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Model output execution without validation
truth: Validate all model outputs before returning them to users or downstream systems; never execute model outputs directly, especially in agent or automation contexts.
quote: Execute model outputs directly, especially in agent or automation contexts. Model output is untrusted until validated.
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: gRPC input validation with Protocol Buffers
truth: Protocol Buffers provide type safety but not business logic validation; always perform thorough server-side validation using protoc-gen-validate or custom checks.
quote: Protocol Buffers provide type safety but not business logic validation
source: OWASP CS: gRPC Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Java ProcessBuilder command string concatenation
truth: Stop passing command and arguments as a single string to ProcessBuilder; pass the command and each argument as separate elements to prevent OS command injection.
quote: the command together with the arguments are passed as a one string, making easy to manipulate that expression and inject malicious strings.
source: OWASP CS: Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Node.js csurf CSRF package
truth: Do not use the csurf package for CSRF protection; it is deprecated with an unfixed security hole and the team recommends using any other CSRF protection package.
quote: a security hole in this package has been recently discovered. The team behind the package has not fixed the discovered vulnerability and they have marked the package as deprecated
source: OWASP CS: NodeJS Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Expiring inactive user passwords during hash upgrade
truth: Do not expire and delete password hashes of inactive users; instead use the existing hashes as inputs for a more secure algorithm and replace them on next login.
quote: Expiring the passwords of many users may cause issues for support staff or may be interpreted by users as an indication of a breach.
source: OWASP CS: Password Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Accept header to Content-Type reflection
truth: Do NOT copy the Accept header to the Content-Type header of the response; reject with 406 Not Acceptable if the Accept header does not contain an allowable type.
quote: - **Do NOT** simply copy the `Accept` header to the `Content-type` header of the response.
source: OWASP CS: REST Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CDATA section normalization by XML parsers
truth: Do not rely on CDATA sections preserving special characters; parsers in recovery mode may normalize CDATA content into escaped entities, altering data integrity.
quote: This means that they will update the special characters contained in the `CDATA` section to contain the safe versions of these characters even though is not required:
source: OWASP CS: XML Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: GCC stack protector default
truth: Do not rely on a bare/legacy -fstack-protector default; the current general-purpose hardening default is -fstack-protector-strong (GCC 4.9+, Debian/Ubuntu/Fedora build default since ~2014) — it protects any function with an array, alloca() call, or address-taken local at modest (~2.4%) size/perf cost. Reserve -fstack-protector-all (instrumenting every function) for extreme-paranoia or legacy codebases where the added overhead is acceptable; it is no longer the recommended general-purpose upgrade.
quote: -fstack-protector guards functions with high risk objects such as C strings, while -fstack-protector-all guards all objects
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA
_audit_fix: STALE: Current GCC/Clang hardening guidance (and most distro defaults) recommends -fstack-protector-strong as the balanced choice — it covers functions with arrays or

subject: encrypting stored keys with envelope encryption
truth: Encrypt stored keys using envelope encryption: a Data Encryption Key (DEK) encrypts the data, and a separately-stored Key Encryption Key (KEK) encrypts the DEK.
quote: encryption keys should themselves be stored in an encrypted form
source: OWASP CS: Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM prompt injection testing
truth: Test defenses against prompt injection using known attack patterns including direct injection, typoglycemia, best-of-N variations, and remote injection vectors.
quote: Test with known attack patterns to verify defenses
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MCP server authorization granularity
truth: Stop using broad OAuth scopes (e.g., full Gmail access) for MCP servers; enforce least-privilege scopes to prevent aggregation risk and confused deputy attacks.
quote: Excessive Permissions / Over-Scoped Tokens
source: OWASP CS: MCP (Model Context Protocol) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Vector database authentication
truth: Enable authentication and use strong credentials on vector databases before production use; they often ship with authentication disabled or optional by default.
quote: Some vector databases or deployment modes may ship with authentication disabled or optional by default.
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: initial login timeout via JavaScript
truth: Use JavaScript in the login page to measure elapsed time since page load and force session ID renewal if login exceeds the timeout, preventing session fixation.
quote: use JavaScript code in the login page to evaluate and measure the amount of time since the page was loaded and a session ID was granted
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: change of authorization method
truth: Stop allowing users to change their authorization method without confirming the current method; require authorization using the current method before switching.
quote: Change of authorization method should be authorized using the current authorization method
source: OWASP CS: Transaction Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: WAF unreliability for XSS prevention
truth: Do not rely on Web Application Firewalls for XSS prevention; WAFs are unreliable, miss client-side DOM XSS, and new bypass techniques are discovered regularly.
quote: WAF’s are unreliable and new bypass techniques are being discovered regularly.
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: JavaScript context after URL context in href
truth: When untrusted data passes through a javascript: protocol handler in an href attribute, encode for both JavaScript and URL contexts using server-side encoding.
quote: In the above example, untrusted data started in the rendering URL context (`href` attribute of an `a` tag) then changed to a JavaScript execution context (`javascript:` protocol handler) which passed
source: OWASP CS: DOM based XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Tenant context injection via request parameters
truth: Stop deriving tenant identity from user-controllable request parameters; derive tenant context from a cryptographically signed token or authentication context.
quote: Manipulating tenant identifiers in requests, tokens, or headers
source: OWASP CS: Multi-Tenant Application Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CI/CD pipeline storing secrets instead of letting consumers retrieve them
truth: Secrets do not necessarily need to be brought to a consumer by a CI/CD pipeline; it is even better when the consumer of the secret retrieves the secret itself.
quote: Secrets do not necessarily need to be brought to a consumer of the secret by a CI/CD pipeline. It is even better when the consumer of the secret retrieves the secret.
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: automatic client logout on idle timeout
truth: Use JavaScript to automatically logout client sessions after idle timeout expires, redirecting to the logout page, and optionally warn the user before timeout.
quote: JavaScript code can be used by the web application in all (or critical) pages to automatically logout client sessions after the idle timeout expires
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: standard CLF HTTP logging for incident response
truth: Stop relying on standard Common Log Format (CLF) for incident response; enable full HTTP audit logging including request URI, headers, body, and response data.
quote: The standard Common Log Format (CLF) utilized by most web servers does not provide adequate data for conducting proper incident response
source: OWASP CS: Virtual Patching Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Java XML parsers (DocumentBuilderFactory, SAXParserFactory, DOM4J) have XXE enabled by default
truth: Disable DTDs entirely by setting 'http://apache.org/xml/features/disallow-doctype-decl' to true on DocumentBuilderFactory, SAXParserFactory, and DOM4J parsers.
quote: Since most Java XML parsers have XXE enabled by default, this language is especially vulnerable to XXE attack, so you must explicitly disable XXE to use these parsers safely.
source: OWASP CS: XML External Entity Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: ASP.NET httpRuntime targetFramework
truth: Set httpRuntime targetFramework to ≥4.5.2 in Web.config; omitting it or setting it lower makes ASP.NET unsafe-by-default regardless of the actual .NET version.
quote: Omitting this tag will also result in unsafe-by-default behavior.
source: OWASP CS: XML External Entity Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CI/CD logging configuration
truth: Configure CI/CD logging to output in a readily parsable format such as JSON or syslog, and send aggregate logs to a centralized log management system or SIEM.
quote: configure the system to log data in a readily parsable format such as JSON or syslog
source: OWASP CS: CI/CD Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: single points of failure in DoS defense
truth: Perform a thorough analysis of components based on functionality, architecture and performance to identify single points of failure before designing defenses.
quote: highlight any single points of system failures, which can range from programming related errors to resource exhaustion
source: OWASP CS: Denial of Service Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CI pipeline failure handling for SBOM
truth: Fail the pipeline if SBOM generation itself fails, but do not fail builds on non-actionable low-severity findings — surface them to triage dashboards instead.
quote: In CI, fail the pipeline if SBOM generation fails, but avoid failing builds on non-actionable low-severity findings — instead surface results to triage dashboards.
source: OWASP CS: Dependency Graph & SBOM Best Practices Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: File upload filename handling
truth: Stop using the original user-supplied filename; change the filename to something generated by the application to prevent path traversal and overwrite attacks.
quote: Change the filename to something generated by the application
source: OWASP CS: File Upload Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM output validation for prompt leakage
truth: Validate LLM outputs against suspicious patterns (system prompt leakage, API key exposure, numbered instructions) and truncate or refuse responses that match.
quote: Monitor LLM outputs for signs of successful injection attacks.
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: payment callback verification
truth: Never trust user redirection parameters for payment verification; always verify payment status server-side with the gateway's API before fulfilling the order.
quote: Assuming user redirection parameters are trustworthy (these are untrusted and can be manipulated)
source: OWASP CS: Secure Integration of Third-Party Payment Gateways Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Legacy systems with no encryption
truth: Stop sending data in plain text from legacy systems; deploy a security proxy or translation gateway that enforces encryption between the client and the proxy.
quote: Many older systems send data in plain text because they were designed for internal networks that were considered secure
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Secondary passwords/PINs/security questions as MFA
truth: Stop treating secondary passwords, PINs, or security questions as multi-factor authentication; they are still 'something you know' and do not constitute MFA.
quote: It must be emphasised that this **does not** constitute multi-factor authentication (as both factors are the same - something you know).
source: OWASP CS: Credential Stuffing Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: allowing CORS from all subdomains via regex
truth: Stop allowing CORS from all subdomains via regex; a subdomain takeover lets an attacker bypass same-origin policy and forge requests with your custom header.
quote: your CORS configuration would allow them to bypass the same origin policy and forge a request with your custom header.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: business logic security in DRF APIs
truth: Use threat models, security design reviews, code reviews, pair programming, and unit tests to catch business logic bugs — automated tools cannot detect them.
quote: business logic bugs are difficult to impossible to detect using automated tools
source: OWASP CS: Django REST Framework (DRF) Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: using JCA/JCE directly for cryptography
truth: Avoid using built-in JCA/JCE classes directly; use a trusted library like Google Tink instead, as JCA/JCE makes it far too easy to make cryptographic errors.
quote: it is strongly recommended to have a cryptography expert review the full design and code, as even the most trivial error can severely weaken your encryption
source: OWASP CS: Java Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: key generation outside cryptographic module
truth: Stop generating cryptographic keys outside a FIPS 140-2/140-3 compliant cryptographic module; keys shall be generated within the cryptographic module itself.
quote: Cryptographic keys shall be generated within cryptographic module with at least a `FIPS 140-2 or 140-3` compliance.
source: OWASP CS: Key Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: service-to-service authentication with token-based online validation
truth: For critical requests, validate tokens online via a centralized token service; for non-critical requests, use offline validation with downloaded public keys.
quote: Should be applied to non-critical requests.
source: OWASP CS: Microservices Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SSRF metadata service IPs not blocked
truth: When a deny-list is unavoidable, block at minimum 169.254.169.254, metadata.amazonaws.com, metadata.google.internal, 127.0.0.0/8, and RFC1918 private ranges.
quote: When unavoidable, block these minimum ranges
source: OWASP CS: Server-Side Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Session ID Length vs Entropy
truth: Ensure the entire session ID is randomly generated and unpredictable; if any part is fixed or predictable, increase length to compensate for reduced entropy.
quote: if any part of the session ID is fixed or predictable, the effective entropy is reduced
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: logging session IDs in plaintext
truth: Stop logging session IDs in plaintext; log a salted-hash of the session ID instead to allow session-specific log correlation without exposing the session ID.
quote: It is recommended to log a salted-hash of the session ID instead of the session ID itself
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XSS via script tag injection
