truth: Stop allowing raw <SCRIPT> tags from user input; apply HTML sanitization that removes or escapes script elements, or use a CSP that disallows inline scripts.
quote: This attack, which uses normal XSS JavaScript injection, serves as a baseline
source: OWASP CS: XSS Filter Evasion Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: DOM-based data skimming in browser extensions
truth: Stop rendering sensitive user information directly into a web page's DOM; display it in extension-controlled UIs like popups, options pages, or side panels.
quote: When an extension renders sensitive user information directly into DOM of a web page, this data becomes accessible to the page's own scripts.
source: OWASP CS: Browser Extension Security Vulnerabilities Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CI/CD credential hygiene
truth: Stop storing plaintext secrets in CI/CD environment variables or pipeline configuration files; use a dedicated secrets manager with short-lived credentials.
quote: Insufficient Credential Hygiene
source: OWASP CS: CI/CD Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: DOM XSS - double encoding from HTML + JS encoding
truth: Stop double-encoding: when setting an HTML attribute in the DOM execution context, apply only JavaScript encoding, not HTML attribute encoding on top of it.
quote: The ESAPI.encoder().encodeForHTMLAttribute() is unnecessary and causes double-encoding
source: OWASP CS: DOM based XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: IDOR — complex identifiers are not a substitute for access control
truth: Stop relying on GUIDs or complex identifiers as the sole protection; always enforce server-side access control checks even when identifiers are unguessable.
quote: even with complex identifiers, access control checks are essential. If attackers obtain URLs for unauthorized objects, the application should still block their access attempts.
source: OWASP CS: Insecure Direct Object Reference Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Missing tenant-scoped audit logging
truth: Stop logging without tenant context; include a tenant identifier in every audit log entry to support regulatory compliance and incident response per tenant.
quote: Insufficient tenant-specific logging for regulatory requirements
source: OWASP CS: Multi-Tenant Application Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: npm package publishing with secrets
truth: Stop relying on .gitignore alone; add a .npmignore or use the 'files' allowlist in package.json to prevent secrets from being published to the npm registry.
quote: Developers may end up updating the `.gitignore` file, but forget to update `.npmignore` as well, which can lead to a potentially sensitive file not being pushed to source control, but still being incl
source: OWASP CS: NPM Security best practices
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XML payload schema validation
truth: Validate SOAP payloads against their XML schema definition (XSD) with maximum length, character set, and allow-list validation patterns for every parameter.
quote: Web services must validate [SOAP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SOAP) payloads against their associated XML schema definition ([XSD](https://www.w3schools.com/xml/schema_intro.asp)).
source: OWASP CS: Web Service Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Python XML parsing modules (sax, etree, minidom, pulldom, xmlrpc)
truth: Stop using Python's stdlib XML parsers without protection; use defusedxml to guard against Billion Laughs, Quadratic Blowup, and decompression bomb attacks.
quote: To protect your application from the applicable attacks, [two packages](https://docs.python.org/3/library/xml.html#the-defusedxml-and-defusedexpat-packages) exist to help you sanitize your input and p
source: OWASP CS: XML External Entity Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Browser cache timing for resource detection
truth: Use unpredictable per-user tokens in resource URLs or set Cache-Control: no-store to prevent attackers from detecting cached resources via load-time timing.
quote: If an attacker is able to detect whether a resource was loaded from the cache after the load time, he will be able to draw some conclusions based on it.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-site leaks Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: OTA update authentication
truth: Stop delivering OTA updates without cryptographic signature verification; implement code signing and verify signatures before applying any firmware update.
quote: OTA updates may lack proper authentication and encryption, allowing attackers to inject malicious firmware
source: OWASP CS: Top 10 Automotive Security Vulnerabilities
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM prompt injection — dual-LLM pattern
truth: Use the dual-LLM pattern: a privileged LLM holds tools but never reads untrusted content; a quarantined LLM reads untrusted content but cannot take action.
quote: A privileged LLM holds the tools but never reads untrusted content directly. A quarantined LLM reads untrusted content but cannot take action.
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Direct write access to vector index from application code
truth: Restrict write access to the vector index to authorized ingestion pipelines only; never allow direct write access from application code or agent endpoints.
quote: Restrict write access to the vector index to authorized ingestion pipelines only. No application code or agent endpoint should have direct write access.
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

associative: app code write to vector db

subject: AWS S3 bucket deletion
truth: Delete the S3 bucket AND remove the DNS CNAME record pointing to it; otherwise any AWS account can recreate the bucket and serve content on your subdomain.
quote: Bucket names are globally unique across all AWS accounts. If a bucket is deleted, any AWS account can recreate it with the same name and serve content on the CNAME.
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: mobile app authentication for vehicle access
truth: Stop using weak or guessable passwords in vehicle mobile apps; enforce multi-factor authentication and strong password policies for remote vehicle access.
quote: If a vehicle’s mobile app uses easily guessable passwords, an attacker could log in and change vehicle settings or track location.
source: OWASP CS: Top 10 Automotive Security Vulnerabilities
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Shadow DOM insufficiency for sensitive data
truth: Do not rely on Shadow DOM (open or closed) to protect sensitive data from page scripts or other extensions; use separate extension-controlled UIs instead.
quote: even using a Shadow DOM for encapsulation may not be a sufficient safeguard, as page scripts can still query an 'open' Shadow DOM
source: OWASP CS: Browser Extension Security Vulnerabilities Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: document_start timing and prototype pollution in iframes
truth: Do not assume native prototypes are safe even at document_start timing; Chromium iframes can be polluted by page scripts before extension scripts execute.
quote: Also, please don't assume your extension's script can use native prototypes even if it's executed at `document_start` timing.
source: OWASP CS: Browser Extension Security Vulnerabilities Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: session cookie theft detection
truth: Store IP, User-Agent, Accept-Language, and Date on the server when a session is established, and compare them on each request to detect session hijacking.
quote: By storing session information on the server side when a session is established, it is possible to detect session hijacking when that information is significantly changed.
source: OWASP CS: Cookie Theft Mitigation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Trusted Types for DOM XSS prevention
truth: Enable Content-Security-Policy: require-trusted-types-for 'script' to force DOM XSS sinks to reject plain strings and eliminate entire classes of DOM XSS.
quote: This causes DOM XSS sinks (`innerHTML`, `outerHTML`, `document.write`, `script.src`, etc.) to reject plain strings, forcing all assignments to go through a vetted policy.
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: ASP.NET Membership default password hashing (SHA-1 single iteration)
truth: Do not rely on the default ASP.NET Membership password storage; it uses a single iteration of SHA-1 which is weak — use ASP.NET Identity (PBKDF2) instead.
quote: The default storage hashes the password with a single iteration of SHA-1 which is rather weak
source: OWASP CS: DotNet Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Kubernetes built-in API authentication
truth: Use external API authentication (OIDC, IAM, or Kubernetes Impersonation) instead of Kubernetes built-in authentication mechanisms for production clusters.
quote: Due to the weakness of Kubernetes' internal mechanisms for authenticating APIs, we strongly recommended that larger or production clusters use one of the external API authentication methods
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM input sanitization
truth: Do not rely on keyword-based filters to block prompt injection; attackers bypass them with typoglycemia-scrambled words, encoding, or Unicode obfuscation.
quote: Exploiting LLMs' ability to read scrambled words where first and last letters remain correct, bypassing keyword-based filters
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MIDDLEWARE to BACKEND access across services
truth: Prohibit access from the MIDDLEWARE segment to the BACKEND segment of another service; never allow a middleware tier to reach a foreign database directly.
quote: Access from the MIDDLEWARE segment to the BACKEND segment of another service is prohibited (access to a foreign database bypassing the application server is prohibited)
source: OWASP CS: Network segmentation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Bring Your Own Key (BYOK) complexity
truth: Stop importing your own key material to cloud KMS unless your threat model and policy strictly require it; do use the cloud provider-managed keys instead.
quote: unless you know what you are doing and your threat model and policy require this, this is not a recommended solution due to its complexity and difficulty of use.
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: transaction authorization vs authentication
truth: Stop using the same method for authentication and transaction authorization; use different methods or different actions in an external security component.
quote: applications should not require a user to perform the same actions for authentication and transaction authorization
source: OWASP CS: Transaction Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

associative: What You See Is What You Sign

subject: onBeforeUnload event defeating frame busting
truth: Do not rely on frame-busting navigation alone; an attacker can register an onBeforeUnload handler that prompts the user to cancel the busting navigation.
quote: To exploit this, the framing page registers an `onBeforeUnload` handler which is called whenever the framing page is about to be unloaded due to navigation.
source: OWASP CS: Clickjacking Defense Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: sandbox iframe disabling frame-busting JavaScript
truth: Do not rely on JavaScript frame-busting alone; the sandbox attribute on an iframe disables JavaScript in the subframe, silently defeating the protection.
quote: If JavaScript is disabled in the context of the subframe, the frame busting code will not run.
source: OWASP CS: Clickjacking Defense Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: accepting text/plain content type for JSON APIs
truth: Stop accepting text/plain content type on JSON APIs; it is a simple content type that bypasses CORS preflight and leaves the endpoint vulnerable to CSRF.
quote: Many modern web applications use JSON APIs so would naturally require CORS, however they may accept `text/plain` which would be vulnerable to CSRF.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: fallback to window/document properties for sensitive operations
truth: Stop using fallback patterns like `window.x || defaultValue` for sensitive operations; attackers can inject HTML elements to override the value silently.
quote: attackers can exploit it by injecting markups with colliding names
source: OWASP CS: DOM Clobbering Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AI assistants in CI/CD pipelines
truth: Limit AI assistant capabilities in workflows — only enable the minimum tools and actions required for task execution to prevent prompt injection attacks.
quote: limit AI assistant capabilities — only enable the minimum tools and actions required for task execution
source: OWASP CS: GitHub Actions Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Node.js Docker base image tag
truth: Stop using bare 'FROM node' or 'FROM node:lts-alpine' without a digest; pin the base image to an exact SHA256 digest for deterministic and secure builds.
quote: Docker image builds are inconsistent
source: OWASP CS: Node.js Docker Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: authorization token change
truth: Stop authorizing token changes without the current token; require the current authorization credentials to authorize a change of the authorization token.
quote: Change of authorization token should be authorized using the current authorization token
source: OWASP CS: Transaction Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Continuous device health monitoring
truth: Set up systems that continuously check device health, updates, and security — devices that aren't secure automatically lose access or get limited access.
quote: Devices that aren't secure automatically lose access or get limited access.
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Browser extension permissions overreach
truth: Stop requesting broad permissions like 'tabs' and 'http://*/*' by default; follow the Principle of Least Privilege and use optional permissions instead.
quote: request more permissions than they actually need
source: OWASP CS: Browser Extension Security Vulnerabilities Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CSP as sole XSS defense
truth: Do not use Content-Security-Policy as the sole XSS defense; CSP must be used as a defense-in-depth technique, not a one-size-fits-all enterprise policy.
quote: Sole Reliance on Content-Security-Policy (CSP) Headers
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: etcd access control
truth: Restrict etcd access to only the API servers using mutual TLS and firewall isolation; do not allow other components to access the primary etcd instance.
quote: Write access to the API server's etcd is equivalent to gaining root on the entire cluster
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Sensitive data asset classification
truth: Identify and classify all sensitive data assets into protection levels (e.g., PII, confidential) and map them to their storages to prevent data leakage.
quote: Identify and describe data assets that processed by system microservices/services. It is advisable firstly to identify assets, which are valuable from a security perspective
source: OWASP CS: Microservices based Security Arch Doc Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Node.js blocking operations with race conditions
truth: Do not perform synchronous operations after an async callback that depends on them; move all dependent operations inside the same non-blocking callback.
quote: Such race conditions can also affect the security of your application.
source: OWASP CS: NodeJS Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: bcrypt null-byte truncation with pre-hashing
truth: Pre-hash the password with a fast hash and encode the output to a printable string (e.g. base64) before passing to bcrypt to avoid null-byte truncation.
quote: The original bcrypt expects a null terminated password string, this means that the hash value will only be used to the first null byte in the hash value.
source: OWASP CS: Password Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
truth: Do not rely on unpredictable or masked IDs to protect resources; perform access control checks on every request for the specific object being accessed.
quote: A user should not be able to access a resource they do not have permissions simply because they are able to guess and manipulate that object's identifier
source: OWASP CS: Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: parent.location navigation in double-framed pages
truth: Do not use parent.location = self.location for frame busting; double framing causes a security violation that silently disables the counter-navigation.
quote: However, if the attacker encloses the victim in one frame inside another (a double frame), then accessing `parent.location` becomes a security violation in all popular browsers, due to the **descendan
source: OWASP CS: Clickjacking Defense Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: IP blocking as sole credential stuffing defense
truth: Do not use IP blocking as the sole or primary defense against credential stuffing; attackers use proxy networks to distribute requests across many IPs.
quote: should not be used as the sole or primary defense due to the ease in circumvention
source: OWASP CS: Credential Stuffing Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: setAttribute with event handler names
truth: Stop using setAttribute(name_string, value_string) to set event handler attributes; set the event handler property directly on the DOM element instead.
quote: The `setAttribute(name_string,value_string)` method is dangerous because it implicitly coerces the *value_string* into the DOM attribute datatype of *name_string*.
source: OWASP CS: DOM based XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: untrusted data on left side of window[] expression
truth: Stop placing untrusted data on the left side of a window[] expression; use it only on the right side to prevent subversion of window object attributes.
quote: Use untrusted data on only the right side of an expression, especially data that looks like code and may be passed to the application (e.g., `location` and `eval()`).
source: OWASP CS: DOM based XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Bluetooth pairing mode
truth: Enforce LE Secure Connections (Bluetooth 4.2+) with Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman; do not use 'Just Works' pairing which is vulnerable to MITM attacks.
quote: - Pairing methods such as [_Just works_](https://devzone.nordicsemi.com/f/nordic-q-a/17165/ble-just-works-pairing) are vulnerable to MITM attacks! Do not use them if you're setting up your own Bluetoo
source: OWASP CS: Drone Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: RAG system document poisoning
truth: Validate and sanitize documents ingested into RAG vector databases; attacker-controlled documents can inject instructions that override system prompts.
quote: Poisoning documents in vector databases with harmful instructions
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Logging authentication failures
truth: Always log authentication successes and failures — failed attempts are critical early indicators of credential-based attacks and must never be omitted.
quote: Authentication successes and failures
source: OWASP CS: Logging Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: NODE_ENV production in Dockerfile
truth: Always set ENV NODE_ENV production in the Dockerfile for production builds to enable performance and security optimizations in frameworks like Express.
quote: Some frameworks and libraries may only turn on the optimized configuration that is suited to production if that `NODE_ENV` environment variable is set to `production`.
source: OWASP CS: Node.js Docker Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: RAG pipeline as black box
truth: Do not treat RAG as a black box — log the full pipeline for every request including query, chunks retrieved, model input, model output, and tool calls.
quote: Treat RAG as a black box where queries go in and answers come out with no visibility into what happened in between.
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Session ID disclosure via URL
truth: Stop transmitting session IDs in URL parameters; use secure cookies or HTTP headers instead to prevent disclosure via referrer logs or browser history.
quote: The disclosure, capture, prediction, brute force, or fixation of the session ID will lead to session hijacking
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Session invalidation on logout/expiration
truth: Call server-side session invalidation functions (HttpSession.invalidate(), Session.Abandon(), session_destroy()) when session expires or user logs out.
quote: In order to close and invalidate the session on the server side, it is mandatory for the web application to take active actions when the session expires, or the user actively logs out, by using the fu
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: caching in release/publishing workflows
truth: Disable all forms of caching in release or publishing workflows to avoid reusing potentially compromised artifacts or exfiltrating production secrets.
quote: disable all forms of caching in release or publishing workflows to avoid reusing potentially compromised artifacts or exfiltrating production secrets
source: OWASP CS: GitHub Actions Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: IDOR via direct object reference in URL path
truth: Stop accessing objects by direct primary key without verifying the user's ownership; instead scope the query to the current user's accessible objects.
quote: This happens because the app didn't properly check if the user had permission to view data for user 124 before displaying it.
source: OWASP CS: Insecure Direct Object Reference Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LDAP distinguished name escaping
truth: Escape DN special characters (\ # + < > , ; " = and leading/trailing spaces) using proper encoding functions like Encoder.LdapDistinguishedNameEncode.
quote: Characters and character sequences that must be excluded from allowlists
source: OWASP CS: LDAP Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: excessive session logging
truth: Log excess_sessions_exceeded when a user exceeds the maximum number of concurrent sessions, as it can indicate credential sharing or account takeover.
quote: An unusually high concurrent-session count can indicate credential sharing or account takeover
source: OWASP CS: Application Logging Vocabulary Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: OAuth 2.0 Bearer Token Replay Prevention
truth: Sender-constrain access tokens using DPoP (RFC 9449) or mTLS (RFC 8705) to cryptographically bind tokens to specific clients and prevent token replay.
quote: implement mechanisms for sender-constraining access tokens, such as Mutual TLS for OAuth 2.0 (mTLS - RFC 8705) or Demonstration of Proof of Possession (DPoP - RFC 9449)
source: OWASP CS: OAuth 2.0 Protocol Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: client_id and sub claims
truth: Authorization Servers must not allow clients to influence their client_id, sub value, or any Claim that can be confused with a genuine Resource Owner.
quote: Authorization Servers do not allow clients to influence their "client_id" or "sub" value or any other Claim that can be confused with a genuine Resource Owner.
source: OWASP CS: OAuth 2.0 Protocol Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Dynamic sort order in SQL queries
truth: When user input controls sort order, convert it to a boolean and use that to select the safe ASC/DESC value rather than appending user input directly.
quote: it is best if the user supplied input is converted to a boolean, and then that boolean is used to select the safe value to append to the query
source: OWASP CS: SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: secrets management access policies
truth: Always create access policies to limit which entities can read or write secrets, and separate production and development secrets management solutions.
quote: Secrets should never be retrievable by everyone and everything. Always make sure that you put guardrails in place
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AI coding agent auto-accept permissions
truth: Stop running AI coding agents with auto-accept enabled and full developer permissions; restrict agent permissions to the minimum needed for each task.
quote: Many developers run these agents with auto-accept enabled, meaning the agent operates with the developer's full permissions and minimal human oversight.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Zero Trust access decisions
truth: Stop granting access based on valid credentials alone; continuously verify every request with risk assessment, device health, and behavioral analysis.
quote: access denied even with valid credentials if risk is high
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Mass Assignment via forceFill
truth: Stop using forceFill with unsanitized request input; qualify allowed parameters using $request->only or $request->validated instead of $request->all.
quote: - Avoid using methods such as `forceFill` or `forceCreate` that bypass the protection mechanism.
source: OWASP CS: Laravel Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MCP message signature verification failure handling
truth: Fail closed when signature verification fails — reject the message entirely; never silently fall back to unsigned processing when signing is enabled.
quote: Fail closed when verification fails. If a signature is missing, invalid, or the nonce has been seen before, reject the message entirely. Never silently fall back to unsigned processing when signing is enabled.
source: OWASP CS: MCP (Model Context Protocol) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Geolocation as MFA factor
truth: Stop using geolocation as a standalone MFA factor; it is easy for an attacker to bypass by obtaining IP addresses in the trusted country or location.
quote: Easy for an attacker to bypass by obtaining IP addresses in the trusted country or location
source: OWASP CS: Multifactor Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: dependency confusion
truth: Always use scoped package names (@yourorg/package-name) for internal packages and configure .npmrc to point scoped packages to your private registry.
quote: - Always use **scoped package names** for internal packages (e.g., `@yourorg/package-name` instead of `package-name`)
source: OWASP CS: NPM Security best practices
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: npm install in Docker image
truth: Stop using 'RUN npm ci' without --omit=dev in production Docker images; install only production dependencies to reduce attack surface and image size.
quote: In the case of building a Docker image for production we want to ensure that we only install production dependencies in a deterministic way, and this brings us to the following recommendation for the
source: OWASP CS: Node.js Docker Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Access control metadata stripped during chunking
truth: Store access control metadata (classification, owner, permitted roles, permitted tenants) alongside every vector chunk, not just the source document.
quote: Strip access control metadata during chunking or embedding
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: model deserialization
truth: Stop using pickle or torch.load on untrusted model files without integrity verification; use safetensors or verify signatures before deserialization.
quote: A `.pt` or `.pkl` file embedded with malware is uploaded to a pipeline and executed during deserialization.
source: OWASP CS: Secure AI/ML Model Ops Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: session ID in URL
truth: Do not include the session ID in the URL; URL-based session IDs are disclosed in web links, logs, browser history, bookmarks, and the Referer header.
quote: The usage of specific session ID exchange mechanisms, such as those where the ID is included in the URL, might disclose the session ID (in web links and logs, web browser history and bookmarks, the Referer header or search engines)
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CORS with Access-Control-Allow-Origin wildcard and credentials
truth: Stop setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin=* when Access-Control-Allow-Credentials=true; the browser will reject the response, silently breaking CORS.
quote: The browser will reject any response that includes `Access-Control-Allow-Origin=*` if credentials are allowed.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: database account permissions
truth: Stop using root/sa/SYS accounts or granting administrative rights; create dedicated accounts with only SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE on specific databases.
quote: - Do not use the built-in `root`, `sa` or `SYS` accounts.
source: OWASP CS: Database Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Reverse Tabnabbing via window.open
truth: Stop calling window.open without noopener,noreferrer in the windowFeatures parameter; always include 'noopener,noreferrer' and reset opener to null.
quote: - For the JavaScript `window.open` function, add the values `noopener,noreferrer` in the [windowFeatures](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Window/open) parameter of the `window.open` f
source: OWASP CS: HTML5 Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM prompt injection — guardrail model susceptibility
truth: Do not rely on a guardrail LLM as a single defense; it is itself susceptible to prompt injection and must be one layer in a defense-in-depth design.
quote: A guardrail LLM is itself an LLM and is itself susceptible to prompt injection.
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MCP server sandboxing
truth: Stop running local MCP servers with full host access; sandbox them to prevent file system traversal, credential theft, and arbitrary code execution.
quote: Local MCP servers running with full host access, enabling file system traversal, credential theft, or arbitrary code execution
source: OWASP CS: MCP (Model Context Protocol) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Service-to-storage access documentation
truth: Document every service-to-storage relation with access type (Read or Read/Write) to enforce least privilege and prevent over-privileged data access.
quote: #### Identify "service-to-storage" relations
source: OWASP CS: Microservices based Security Arch Doc Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: npm install without frozen lockfile
truth: Stop using npm install or yarn install in CI/build; use npm ci or yarn install --frozen-lockfile to enforce the lockfile and abort on inconsistency.
quote: When they detect an inconsistency between the project’s `package.json` and the lockfile, they compensate for such change based on the `package.json` manifest by installing different versions than thos
source: OWASP CS: NPM Security best practices
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: npm publish without dry-run
truth: Stop publishing without first reviewing the tarball; add --dry-run to npm publish to inspect what files will be included before actually publishing.
quote: To be extra careful, add a `--dry-run` command-line argument to your publish command in order to first review how the tarball is created without actually publishing it to the registry.
source: OWASP CS: NPM Security best practices
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: documentation examples copied into production
truth: Never copy README examples into production without a security review; treat documentation code as a starting point, not a production-ready solution.
quote: Never copy README examples into production without a security review.
source: OWASP CS: NPM Security best practices
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Session ID entropy and randomness
truth: Generate session IDs with sufficient entropy using a cryptographically secure random number generator to prevent prediction and brute force attacks.
quote: prediction, brute force, or fixation of the session ID will lead to session hijacking
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CSP hash-based script allowlisting
truth: Using hashes for CSP is risky because any change to whitespace or formatting inside the script tag changes the hash and silently breaks the script.
quote: If you change *anything* inside the script tag (even whitespace) by, e.g., formatting your code, the hash will be different, and the script won't render.
source: OWASP CS: Content Security Policy Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: writing custom cryptographic functions
truth: Never write your own cryptographic functions; always use a trusted, well-known implementation library like Google Tink instead of JCA/JCE directly.
quote: Never, ever write your own cryptographic functions.
source: OWASP CS: Java Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: password storage with fast hashing algorithms
truth: Stop using fast hashing algorithms like SHA-256 for password storage; use slow, memory-hard algorithms such as Argon2id, bcrypt, or PBKDF2 instead.
quote: Fast hashing algorithms such as SHA‑256 are not suitable for password storage because they allow attackers to perform large numbers of guesses quickly.
source: OWASP CS: Password Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Missing X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on API responses
truth: Include X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff in API responses to prevent browsers from MIME sniffing and inappropriately interpreting responses as HTML.
quote: This header with a `nosniff` value prevents browsers from performing MIME sniffing, and inappropriately interpreting responses as HTML.
source: OWASP CS: REST Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CI/CD credentials without rotation and expiry
truth: Credentials used by CI/CD tooling to authenticate against the secret management system must be rotated frequently and expire after a job completes.
quote: Rotation/Temporality: credentials used by the CI/CD tooling to authenticate against the secret management system are rotated frequently and expire after a job completes.
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Axios CSRF token default headers per-method vs interceptor
truth: Use axios interceptors instead of per-method default headers to ensure CSRF tokens are added for all state-changing methods including custom ones.
quote: Alternative: Using interceptors for all requests
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Interceptor missing DOM-based XSS and non-parameter taint
truth: HTTP interceptors cannot prevent DOM-based XSS and miss tainted data from request headers, extra path info, internal REST services, and databases.
quote: not effective against DOM-based XSS
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: BinaryFormatter deserialization in .NET
truth: Stop using BinaryFormatter for data processing; use XmlSerializer, DataContractSerializer, BinaryReader/BinaryWriter, or System.Text.Json instead.
quote: Use the BinaryFormatter type which is dangerous and [not recommended](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/serialization/binaryformatter-security-guide) for data processing.
source: OWASP CS: DotNet Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: File upload Content-Type header validation
truth: Stop trusting the Content-Type header for file type validation; validate the actual file content using magic bytes or a proper file type detector.
quote: - **Validate the file type, don't trust the [Content-Type header](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Type) as it can be spoofed**
source: OWASP CS: File Upload Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MFA reset/bypass procedures
truth: Implement a secure procedure to allow users to reset their MFA; processes that allow users to bypass or reset MFA may be exploitable by attackers.
quote: Processes implemented to allow users to bypass or reset MFA may be exploitable by attackers
source: OWASP CS: Multifactor Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: unanchored regex patterns in documentation
truth: Stop copying unanchored regex patterns like /example\.com/ from documentation; use anchored patterns like ^https:\/\/example\.com$ for validation.
quote: Libraries that accept regex patterns for validation (e.g., JWT audience matching, CORS origin matching) show examples with unanchored patterns like `/example\.com/`, which match `malicious-example.com
source: OWASP CS: NPM Security best practices
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: syslog log shipping for tamper-proof logging
truth: Copy logs to a separate server using the syslog protocol, which only allows adding new events and prevents attackers from modifying existing logs.
quote: copy the logs to a separate server, for example, using the syslog protocol, which does not allow an attacker to modify the logs, syslog only allows you to add new events to the logs
source: OWASP CS: Network segmentation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: OAuth 2.0 open redirectors
truth: Stop exposing URLs that forward the user's browser to arbitrary URIs obtained from a query parameter; use an allowlist of permitted redirect URIs.
quote: Clients and Authorization Server must not expose URLs that forward the user's browser to arbitrary URIs obtained from a query parameter ("open redirectors") which can enable exfiltration of authorization codes and access tokens.
source: OWASP CS: OAuth 2.0 Protocol Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: allowlist input validation for OS command arguments
truth: Validate command arguments using positive allowlist input validation with a regular expression that only allows safe characters and limits length.
quote: Where are the arguments allowed explicitly defined.
source: OWASP CS: OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: secrets management security monitoring
truth: Continually monitor who accesses secrets, from which IP, and what methodology; alert if CI/CD credentials access the secret from an unexpected IP.
quote: Monitor who accesses the secret at the secret management system: is this normal behavior?
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Autotools ship insecure compiler/linker flags by default
truth: Do not assume Autotools enable security hardening flags; explicitly add -fPIE, -pie, -z noexecstack, -z relro, -z now, and -fstack-protector-all.
quote: miss many security related opportunities and ship insecure out of the box
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Server-side validation of fixed-set inputs
truth: Validate drop-down and radio button inputs against the exact allowed set on the server side, and log mismatches as high-severity security events.
quote: Any failure to validate a value against this discrete list of options on the server side is a high security event and should be logged as a high severity event
source: OWASP CS: Input Validation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Source IP allow-list as MFA factor
truth: Stop using source IP address as a standalone MFA factor; it provides no protection if the user's system is compromised or against rogue insiders.
quote: Doesn't provide any protection if the user's system is compromised
source: OWASP CS: Multifactor Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

associative: allow list IP addresses for MFA

subject: Missing .dockerignore in multi-stage builds
truth: Without a .dockerignore, COPY . in the production stage copies local node_modules and sensitive files (like .env, aws.json) into the final image.
quote: we may also be copying into the Docker image sensitive files that include credentials or local configuration
code_bad:
FROM node:lts-alpine
RUN apk add dumb-init
ENV NODE_ENV production
USER node
WORKDIR /usr/src/app
COPY --chown=node:node --from=build /usr/src/app/node_modules /usr/src/app/node_modules
COPY --chown=node:node . /usr/src/app
CMD ["dumb-init", "node", "server.js"]
source: OWASP CS: Node.js Docker Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SSRF domain validation without DNS resolution
truth: Use libraries that validate domain name format without performing DNS resolution queries to avoid information disclosure and DNS pinning attacks.
quote: Verification of the proposed libraries has been performed to ensure that the proposed functions do not perform any DNS resolution query.
source: OWASP CS: Server-Side Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: session ID exchange mechanisms
truth: Accept only cookies for session ID exchange; reject session IDs submitted via URL parameters, hidden form fields, or other non-cookie mechanisms.
quote: If a user submits a session ID through a different exchange mechanism, such as a URL parameter, the web application should avoid accepting it as part of a defensive strategy to stop session fixation.
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XS-Leaks via frame counting and navigation
truth: Stop assuming SameSite cookies fully prevent XS-Leaks; window-based attacks like frame counting and navigation still leak cross-site information.
quote: SameSite cookies are a strong **defense-in-depth** mechanism against **some** classes of XS Leaks and [CSRF attacks](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Preventio
source: OWASP CS: Cross-site leaks Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: OS package managers in container images
truth: Do not include OS package managers or shells in container images; if OS packages are required, remove the package manager in a later build step.
quote: do not include OS package managers or shells in CIs because they may have unknown vulnerabilities
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM prompt injection — guardrail drift monitoring
truth: Log every guardrail decision and watch for drift; sudden changes in approval rate or refusal-reason distribution often precede a working bypass.
quote: Log every guardrail decision and watch for drift. Sudden changes in the approval rate, or in the distribution of refusal reasons, often precede a working bypass.
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM system prompt design
truth: Design system prompts with clear role definitions and security constraints, and use structured prompt formats separating instructions from data.
quote: Design system prompts with clear role definitions and security constraints
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: read-only accounts with views
truth: Stop granting direct table access to read-only accounts; create views that limit access to specific fields and grant access to the view instead.
quote: consider creating a view that limits access to that portion of the data and assigning the account access to the view instead of the underlying table
source: OWASP CS: SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AI agent runtime sandboxing
truth: Run AI agents in sandboxed environments with restricted filesystem, network, and system call access to prevent broken-access-control failures and keep the agent's actions within well-defined authorization boundaries.
quote: Agent Runtime Sandboxing
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: transaction authorization second factor
truth: Do not treat transaction authorization as a simple login session check; require a fresh second-factor verification for each sensitive operation.
quote: applications will require users to submit a second factor so the system can check whether they are authorized to perform a sensitive operation
source: OWASP CS: Transaction Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: WebSocket input sanitization
truth: Sanitize and validate all data received over WebSocket messages server-side, as they can carry XSS, SQL injection, and other malicious payloads.
quote: WebSocket messages can carry XSS, SQL injection, and other malicious payloads
source: OWASP CS: WebSocket Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SubResource token protection against error-event XS-Leaks
truth: Protect sensitive endpoints with long, unique tokens validated server-side to prevent cross-origin resource detection via onload/onerror events.
quote: mechanism of special unique tokens may be implemented to protect our sensitive endpoints
source: OWASP CS: Cross-site leaks Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Zero Trust network location trust
truth: Stop trusting based on network location; treat all communication as untrusted regardless of whether it originates inside or outside the network.
quote: Network location doesn't determine trust level
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Zero Trust legacy system protection
truth: Do not forget legacy systems; they often contain the most sensitive data and become the weakest links requiring a dedicated protection strategy.
quote: Forgetting about legacy systems
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: DOM XSS - HTML attribute encoding in execution context
truth: In a DOM execution context, JavaScript-encode untrusted data before inserting into non-executing HTML attributes; do NOT HTML-attribute-encode.
quote: using HTML Attribute encoding in an execution context will break the application display of data
source: OWASP CS: DOM based XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: input validation discrete fail logging
truth: Log input_validation_discrete_fail when server-side validation of a value against a discrete list fails, as it indicates client-side tampering.
quote: strong indication of malicious activity as this indicates the client-side code has been tampered with
source: OWASP CS: Application Logging Vocabulary Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: manual code review vs automated tools
truth: Do not rely solely on automated SAST/DAST tools; perform manual code review to catch business logic flaws and context-specific vulnerabilities.
quote: **Manual Code Review** complements automated security testing tools (SAST/DAST) by focusing on areas where human analysis provides the most value, including business logic validation, complex security
source: OWASP CS: Secure Code Review Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: version-controlled DNS management
truth: Use version-controlled DNS management (Terraform, OctoDNS, DNSControl) so all record additions and removals are reviewed, approved, and logged.
quote: Use version-controlled DNS management (e.g., Terraform, OctoDNS, or DNSControl) so all record additions and removals are reviewed, approved, and logged.
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: allowlist virtual patch for integer parameters
truth: For parameters expected to contain only integers, create an allowlist virtual patch that verifies the payload contains only integer characters.
quote: In this case, the `reqID` parameter is supposed to only hold integer characters so we can use this virtual patch:
source: OWASP CS: Virtual Patching Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Error handling - generic error responses for REST APIs
truth: Use RFC 7807 Problem Details for HTTP APIs to return generic 5xx errors without implementation details, and log all error details server-side.
quote: use 5xx errors only to indicate responses to requests that it cannot fulfill, but not provide any content as part of the response that would reveal implementation details
source: OWASP CS: Error Handling Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Microservice API definition with security schemes
truth: Define security schemes (scopes, API keys) in every microservice API definition (e.g., OpenAPI spec) instead of leaving endpoints unprotected.
quote: It is advisable to define used security scheme, e.g. define scopes or API keys needed to invoke dedicated endpoint
source: OWASP CS: Microservices based Security Arch Doc Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: RAG source attribution failure
truth: If source attribution cannot be generated, block the response — an unattributed response in a regulated environment is a compliance violation.
quote: An unattributed response in a regulated environment is a compliance violation.
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Missing Cache-Control: no-store on API responses
truth: Include Cache-Control: no-store in all API responses consumed by browser clients to prevent sensitive information from being cached or stored.
quote: Providing `no-store` indicates that any caches of any kind (private or shared) should not store the response that contains the header.
source: OWASP CS: REST Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Rails security scanning
truth: Use static analysis tools like Brakeman or Bearer to identify security issues in Rails applications instead of relying on manual review alone.
quote: Use [brakeman](https://brakemanscanner.org/), an open source code analysis tool for Rails applications, to identify many potential issues.
source: OWASP CS: Ruby on Rails Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MCP server tool descriptions as injection surface
truth: Review tool descriptions for hidden instructions; tool descriptions are part of the agent's context and can contain prompt injection payloads.
quote: Review tool descriptions for hidden instructions. Tool descriptions are part of the agent's context and can contain prompt injection payloads.
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CSS files accessible via forced browsing across roles
truth: Ensure CSS files are access-controlled per role; if a Student can access AdministratorStyling.CSS via forced browsing, log an intrusion alert.
quote: If an authenticated user with the **Student** Role tries to access `AdministratorStyling.CSS` through forced browsing, an alert that an intrusion is occurring should be recorded.
source: OWASP CS: Securing Cascading Style Sheets Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Lucee XML external entities before 5.4.2
truth: Set this.xmlFeatures with externalGeneralEntities:false, secure:true, and disallowDoctypeDecl:true in Application.cfc to disable XXE in Lucee.
quote: you can disable support for XML external entities by adding the following to your Application.cfc
source: OWASP CS: XML External Entity Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CSRF token expiration with timestamps
truth: Stop including timestamps in CSRF tokens for expiration; CSRF tokens are not access tokens and should be tied to the session, not time-based.
quote: It's a common misconception to include timestamps as a value to specify the CSRF token expiration time.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: global variable declarations via id/name attributes
truth: Do not rely on id or name attributes of HTML elements to define global variables; use explicit variable declarations with let, const, or var.
quote: When creating the DOM tree, browsers also create an attribute for (some) named HTML elements on `window` and `document` objects.
source: OWASP CS: DOM Clobbering Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: object[x] accessor with untrusted input
truth: Stop using untrusted input directly as an object property key via object[x] accessors; validate or restrict the key to a known set of values.
quote: ### GUIDELINE \#8 - Limit access to object properties when using object\[x\] accessors
source: OWASP CS: DOM based XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: WCF security modes
truth: Use at least two security modes for WCF bindings — combine message security with transport security (SSL) via TransportWithMessageCredential.
quote: Use at least two security modes for your binding. Message security includes security provisions in the headers. Transport security means use of SSL.
source: OWASP CS: DotNet Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Reverse Tabnabbing via target=_blank
truth: Stop opening links with target=_blank without rel='noopener noreferrer'; add rel='noopener noreferrer' to every link that opens a new window.
quote: - For [HTML links](https://www.scaler.com/topics/html/html-links/), add the attribute `rel="noopener noreferrer"` to every link.
source: OWASP CS: HTML5 Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: IaC resource tagging
truth: Tag all cloud assets during IaC provisioning; untagged assets become ghost resources that cause drift, billing waste, and observability loss.
quote: untagged assets are most likely to result in ghost resources that make it difficult to detect, visualize, and gain observability within the cloud environment
source: OWASP CS: Infrastructure as Code Security Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: session tokens removed from requests during testing
truth: Attempt to execute backend server functionality anonymously by removing any session tokens from POST/GET requests during penetration testing.
quote: Attempt to execute backend server functionality anonymously by removing any session tokens from POST/GET requests.
source: OWASP CS: Mobile Application Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: rogue CA certificate from trusted CA
truth: An attacker can acquire a rogue digital certificate from a trusted CA in the name of the victim site, bypassing SSL/TLS without user warning.
quote: An attacker is able to acquire a rogue digital certificate from a trusted certificate authority (CA) in the name of the victim site
source: OWASP CS: Pinning Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SAML KeyInfo from untrusted document elements
truth: Ignore any KeyInfo elements in the SAML document; obtain signing keys directly from the identity provider via StaticKeySelector or local JKS.
quote: Obtain the key directly from the identity provider, store it in a local file and ignore any `KeyInfo` elements in the document.
source: OWASP CS: SAML Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: PR content passed unsanitized to CI agents
truth: Filter and sanitise PR title, body, comments, and diff before passing them to CI agents as context — PR content is attacker-controlled input.
quote: PR content is attacker-controlled input
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: PHP XML parser (libxml2) before PHP 8.0
truth: On current PHP (8.0+, which bundles libxml2 >= 2.9), external entity loading is disabled by default for DOMDocument, SimpleXML, and XMLReader -- libxml_disable_entity_loader() is now a deprecated no-op. XXE risk only reappears if you explicitly pass LIBXML_NOENT, LIBXML_DTDLOAD, LIBXML_DTDATTR, or LIBXML_DTDVALID to the parser (or on PHP < 8.0 / libxml < 2.9, where the old default still applies and libxml_disable_entity_loader(true) or libxml_set_external_entity_loader(null) is required). Avoid those flags; if you must support PHP < 8.0 or want defense-in-depth, call libxml_set_external_entity_loader(null) explicitly rather than relying on the deprecated libxml_disable_entity_loader(). On PHP 8.4+, the new LIBXML_NO_XXE constant/flag gives explicit, flag-proof XXE protection.
quote: the following should be set when using the default PHP XML parser in order to prevent XXE
source: OWASP CS: XML External Entity Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA
_audit_fix: STALE: As of libxml2 2.9+ (bundled with PHP 8.0 and later, and libxml_disable_entity_loader() is now a deprecated no-op), external entity loading is disabled by defaul

subject: Re-authentication for sensitive features
truth: Require current credential verification before updating sensitive account information (password, email) or executing sensitive transactions.
quote: require the current credentials for an account before updating sensitive account information such as the user's password or email address
source: OWASP CS: Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: authorization vs authentication distinction
truth: Do NOT assume authentication equals authorization; always verify that an authenticated user is approved for the specific action or resource.
quote: Authorization is distinct from authentication which is the process of verifying an entity's identity.
source: OWASP CS: Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

associative: check if user is logged in
user is authenticated so allow access

subject: Validate Permissions on Every Request
truth: Validate permissions correctly on every request regardless of source (AJAX, server-side, etc.) using global, application-wide configuration.
quote: Permission should be validated correctly on every request, regardless of whether the request was initiated by an AJAX script, server-side, or any other source
source: OWASP CS: Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: ignoring snprintf return value
truth: Always check snprintf's return value for truncation instead of ignoring it, or the truncated path may point to an adversary-controlled file.
quote: You should not make them silent truncations by ignoring the warning or casting to `void`:
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CI/CD config file stored alongside source code
truth: Store the CI config file outside the repository hosting the code being built, or ensure it is reviewed before any merge request is approved.
quote: store the CI config file outside the repository that is hosting the code being built
source: OWASP CS: CI/CD Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Django safe filter and mark_safe
truth: Avoid using the safe filter or mark_safe function to disable Django's automatic template escaping unless the input is from a trusted source.
quote: - Try to avoid using the `safe` filter (or `mark_safe` function) to disable Django's automatic template escaping.
source: OWASP CS: Django Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: email canonicalization
truth: Normalize the domain portion to lowercase and store both the original input and a canonical form for consistent comparison across all flows.
quote: Normalize the domain portion to lowercase
source: OWASP CS: Email Validation and Verification in Identity Systems Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: IDOR via hidden form field user_id
truth: Stop trusting user-supplied identifiers in POST bodies or hidden fields; derive the authenticated user from the server-side session instead.
quote: If the app doesn't perform proper access control on the server-side, attackers can manipulate the "user_id" field to modify profiles of other users without authorization.
source: OWASP CS: Insecure Direct Object Reference Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Pod Security Admission
truth: Stop using deprecated Pod Security Policy; use Pod Security Admission with at least the 'baseline' level, and prefer the 'restricted' level.
quote: The previously recommended [Pod Security Policy](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/pod-security-policy/) is deprecated and replaced by [Pod Security Admission](https://kubernetes.io/docs/conc
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Docker .dockerignore for Node.js images
truth: Always include a .dockerignore file to prevent node_modules, .env, credentials, and sensitive files from being copied into the Docker image.
quote: /usr/src/app` from the 2nd Dockerfile stage, we’re also copying over any local node\_modules/ to the Docker image.
code_bad:
FROM node@sha256:b2da3316acdc2bec442190a1fe10dc094e7ba4121d029cb32075ff59bb27390a
WORKDIR /usr/src/app
COPY . /usr/src/app
RUN npm ci
CMD "npm" "start"
source: OWASP CS: Node.js Docker Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: PHP escapeshellcmd allows argument injection via extra parameters
truth: Do not rely on escapeshellcmd() to prevent argument injection; it only prevents command chaining but allows extra parameters to be injected.
quote: `escapeshellcmd()` will still allow this extra parameter meaning the attacker can override the original `--directory-prefix` option, save the file in the current directory and then achieve remote comm
source: OWASP CS: OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: work factor upgrade
truth: Do not force all users to re-hash immediately; wait until the user next authenticates, then re-hash their password with the new work factor.
quote: The most common approach to upgrading the work factor is to wait until the user next authenticates, then re-hash their password with the new work factor.
source: OWASP CS: Password Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Password hashing library must accept NULL byte and full Unicode
truth: Ensure your password hashing library accepts input containing NULL bytes and all Unicode codepoints so user entropy is not silently reduced.
quote: password hashing libraries need to be able to use input that may contain a NULL byte
source: OWASP CS: Password Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SAML XML Signature Wrapping via Reference URI
truth: Verify the ds:Reference URI in the XML signature covers the saml:Assertion element being trusted to mitigate XML Signature Wrapping attacks.
quote: - Verify the `<ds:Reference URI>` in the XML signature covers the `<saml:Assertion>` element being trusted.
source: OWASP CS: SAML Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Session fixation prevention
truth: Do not accept session IDs from URL parameters or unauthenticated sources; generate a new session ID upon authentication to prevent fixation.
quote: fixation of the session ID will lead to session hijacking
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Unvalidated redirect via user-supplied URL parameter
truth: Stop redirecting to user-supplied URL parameters without validation; use a whitelist of allowed destinations or an indirect mapping instead.
quote: The following Java code receives the URL from the parameter named `url` ([GET or POST](https://docs.oracle.com/javaee/7/api/javax/servlet/ServletRequest.html#getParameter-java.lang.String-)) and redir
source: OWASP CS: Unvalidated Redirects and Forwards Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: open redirect via user-supplied URL
truth: Stop accepting user-supplied URLs directly for redirect destinations; use an allow-list of trusted URLs or server-side token-to-URL mapping.
quote: have the user provide short name, ID or token which is mapped server-side to a full target URL
source: OWASP CS: Unvalidated Redirects and Forwards Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: WebSocket monitoring and logging
truth: Implement dedicated logging and monitoring for WebSocket message traffic, as traditional HTTP logs only capture the initial upgrade request.
quote: Traditional HTTP logs only capture the initial upgrade request, missing all message traffic
source: OWASP CS: WebSocket Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Client-Side Authorization Checks
truth: Never rely on client-side access control checks; authorization must be enforced server-side, at the gateway, or using serverless functions.
quote: Developers must never rely on client-side access control checks
source: OWASP CS: Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: PIE and DEP flags dismissed by project Makefile
truth: Stop using project Makefiles that dismiss user-supplied CFLAGS/LDFLAGS; ensure PIE (-fPIE -pie) and DEP (-z noexecstack) flags are honored.
quote: Dismissing user settings combined with insecure out of the box settings (and not picking them up during auto-setup or auto-configure) means a program built with the following will likely have neither defense
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: C/C++ assert calls abort() instead of breaking into debugger
truth: Replace standard assert() with a custom ASSERT that raises SIGTRAP so the debugger snaps at the point of first failure instead of aborting.
quote: Your assert will exchange auto-aborting behavior for auto-debugging behavior. The auto-debugging facility will ensure the debugger snaps when a problem is detected
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CSP as sole XSS defense
truth: Do not rely on CSP as the only defense against XSS; always follow proper output encoding and input validation first, then layer CSP on top.
quote: You must still follow good development practices such as the ones described in [Cross-Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet](Cross_Site_Scripting_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.md), and then deploy CSP on top
source: OWASP CS: Content Security Policy Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: assigning event handlers with JavaScript-encoded strings
truth: Do not assign event handler properties to JavaScript-encoded strings; assign them to a function reference or a valid variable name instead.
quote: The following does NOT work because the event handler is being set to a string.
source: OWASP CS: DOM based XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Java ObjectInputStream subclassing with resolveClass()
truth: Override ObjectInputStream.resolveClass() to restrict which classes are allowed to be deserialized instead of using a generic readObject().
quote: The general idea is to override [`ObjectInputStream.html#resolveClass()`](http://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/java/io/ObjectInputStream.html#resolveClass(java.io.ObjectStreamClass)) in order to r
source: OWASP CS: Deserialization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: ASP.NET Web API error handler returning exception details
truth: Stop leaking exception details in error responses; log the exception server-side and return a generic JSON response with a generic message.
quote: Exception exception = context.Exception;
source: OWASP CS: Error Handling Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: GitHub Actions workflow approval for external contributors
truth: Enable 'Require approval for all external contributors' to ensure workflows triggered by pull requests from forks do not run automatically.
quote: This ensures that workflows triggered by pull requests from forks (i.e., users who are not members of the repository or organization) do not run automatically and therefore prevents untrusted code exe
source: OWASP CS: GitHub Actions Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: JWT token sidejacking — fingerprint context
truth: Bind the JWT to a hardened cookie containing a random fingerprint; store a SHA-256 hash of the fingerprint in the token, not the raw value.
quote: One way to prevent this is by adding a "user context" to the token.
source: OWASP CS: JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: logging security events without standard vocabulary
truth: Use the standardized event vocabulary (AUTHN, AUTHZ, etc.) consistently across all applications so monitoring teams can key on these terms.
quote: specific keywords which, when applied consistently across software, would allow groups to simply monitor for these events terms across all applications
source: OWASP CS: Application Logging Vocabulary Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Tenant ID from request header
truth: Stop trusting client-supplied tenant IDs from request headers; derive tenant from the authenticated session or verified JWT claims instead.
quote: Never trust client-supplied tenant IDs without validation
source: OWASP CS: Multi-Tenant Application Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MFA on sensitive actions
truth: Require MFA not only at login but also for sensitive actions like changing passwords, changing email, disabling MFA, and session elevation.
quote: it may also be appropriate to require MFA for performing sensitive actions
source: OWASP CS: Multifactor Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: redirect URI scheme
truth: Authorization Servers must not allow redirect URIs that use the http scheme except for native clients using Loopback Interface Redirection.
quote: Authorization Servers must not allow redirect URIs that use the "http" scheme except for native clients that use Loopback Interface Redirection.
source: OWASP CS: OAuth 2.0 Protocol Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: API keys in URL query parameters
truth: Stop putting API keys, passwords, or security tokens in URL query parameters; transfer them in request headers or the request body instead.
quote: Passwords, security tokens, and API keys should not appear in the URL, as this can be captured in web server logs
source: OWASP CS: REST Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: DNS records pointing to deprovisioned cloud resources
truth: Stop leaving dangling DNS records when deprovisioning cloud resources; always remove the DNS record as part of the decommissioning process.
quote: Cloud resources are temporary, DNS records are persistent
source: OWASP CS: Subdomain Takeover Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: unvalidated redirects and forwards
truth: Do not allow the URL as user input for the destination; use server-side mapping of short names, IDs, or tokens to full target URLs instead.
quote: do not allow the URL as user input for the destination
source: OWASP CS: Unvalidated Redirects and Forwards Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: committing .npmrc and lockfiles to source control
truth: Commit package manager configuration files (e.g., .npmrc) and lockfiles to source control so CI/CD enforces pinned, verified dependencies.
quote: Finally, regardless of the platform used, ensure the file(s) responsible for controlling these settings (such as `.npmrc` in node environments), is committed to source control and accessible in the CI
source: OWASP CS: CI/CD Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: RAG document ingestion without integrity verification
truth: Hash every document at ingestion time (SHA-256 minimum) and verify the hash before retrieval; reject documents that fail integrity checks.
quote: Hash every document at ingestion time (SHA-256 minimum) and store the hash alongside the document metadata.
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Access control checked only at ingestion, not retrieval
truth: Enforce access control checks at retrieval time, not just at ingestion time. Permissions may have changed since the document was ingested.
quote: Document-level permissions are checked at ingestion but not at retrieval time, allowing permission changes to be ignored.
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: separate DB users per application
truth: Stop using the same owner/admin account across web applications; use different designated database user accounts for each web application.
quote: avoid using the same owner/admin account in the web applications to connect to the database
source: OWASP CS: SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

associative: single db user for all apps

subject: storing secrets in immutable String objects
truth: Do not use immutable structures such as Strings to store secrets; use byte arrays or char arrays where memory can be directly overwritten.
quote: do not use immutable structures such as Strings to store secrets, since it is impossible to force them to be garbage collected
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XML content validation against bombs and XXE
truth: Validate XML input against malformed entities, XML Bomb attacks, external entity attacks (XXE), and use a strong allowlist for all inputs.
quote: Like any web application, web services need to validate input before consuming it
source: OWASP CS: Web Service Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: implicit trust inside network perimeter
truth: Stop trusting users based on location, role, or tenure inside the network; verify every action regardless of where the request originates.
quote: no implicit trust
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Forgotten identities in CI/CD environments
truth: Maintain an accurate, comprehensive, and up-to-date inventory of identities to prevent forgotten identities from becoming attack vectors.
quote: the "forgotten" identity can be the vector an attacker users to compromise a CI/CD system.
source: OWASP CS: CI/CD Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: unencrypted database connections
truth: Stop using unencrypted database connections; configure the database to only allow encrypted connections with TLSv1.2+ and modern ciphers.
quote: Configure the database to only allow encrypted connections
source: OWASP CS: Database Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SBOM format choice
truth: Use standard formats (SPDX or CycloneDX) and publish at least one machine-readable SBOM per release; avoid ad-hoc or proprietary formats.
quote: Use standard formats (SPDX or CycloneDX) and publish at least one machine-readable SBOM per release.
source: OWASP CS: Dependency Graph & SBOM Best Practices Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Legacy code injection flaws are easy to discover in code review
truth: Stop relying on black-box testing alone; examine source code to find injection flaws since they are easy to discover when reviewing code.
