quote: Injection flaws are easy to discover when examining code, but more difficult via testing.
source: OWASP CS: Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: logging PII without considering data privacy
truth: Treat all optional fields as subject to data privacy review; consider the value of logged fields versus responsibility as a data steward.
quote: All fields logged after event type should be considered optional. Businesses implementing this logging method should consider value of the fields being logged vs. responsibility as a data steward for the data logged.
source: OWASP CS: Application Logging Vocabulary Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Missing Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'none' on API responses
truth: Include Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'none' in API responses consumed by browser clients to prevent clickjacking via framing.
quote: Providing `frame-ancestors 'none'` prevents any domain from framing the response returned by the API call.
source: OWASP CS: REST Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: certificate domain name attribute
truth: Place the primary FQDN in the subjectAlternativeName (SAN) attribute, not only in the commonName (CN), because modern browsers ignore CN.
quote: However, modern versions of Chrome ignore the CN attribute, and require that the FQDN is in the `subjectAlternativeName` (SAN) attribute.
source: OWASP CS: Transport Layer Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CI/CD pipeline security risk awareness
truth: Do not treat CI/CD pipelines as low-risk automation; treat them as high-privilege attack surfaces requiring dedicated security controls.
quote: CI/CD pipelines are also an appealing target for malicious hackers, and their security cannot be ignored
source: OWASP CS: CI/CD Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: storing encryption keys in environment variables
truth: Avoid storing keys in environment variables, as they can be accidentally exposed through functions like phpinfo() or /proc/self/environ.
quote: - Avoid storing keys in environment variables, as these can be accidentally exposed through functions such as [phpinfo()](https://www.php.net/manual/en/function.phpinfo.php) or through the `/proc/self
source: OWASP CS: Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Storing uploaded files inside the webroot with read access
truth: Store uploaded files on a different host or outside the webroot; if inside webroot, set write-only permissions and restrict read access.
quote: Store the files on a **different host**, which allows for complete segregation of duties between the application serving the user, and the host handling file uploads and their storage.
source: OWASP CS: File Upload Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM security logging and monitoring
truth: Configure comprehensive logging for all LLM interactions and set up monitoring and alerting for suspicious patterns and usage anomalies.
quote: Configure comprehensive logging for all LLM interactions
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: caching authorization decisions at microservice level
truth: Do not cache authorization decisions at the microservice level; cache the authorization policy instead to avoid applying outdated rules.
quote: caching authorization decisions may lead to applying outdated authorization rules and access control violations
source: OWASP CS: Microservices Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: runtime integrity checks for mobile app
truth: Implement runtime anti-tampering controls that verify app signatures, detect debugging/hooking, and check for rooted/jailbroken devices.
quote: Implement runtime anti-tampering controls
source: OWASP CS: Mobile Application Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: proprietary TOTP tokens
truth: Stop using proprietary/vendor-specific TOTP variants (e.g., Symantec); use standardized TOTP so users can install any authenticator app.
quote: This should be avoided in favour of a standards-based approach.
source: OWASP CS: Multifactor Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SQL Injection prevention via parameterized queries
truth: Stop building SQL queries with string concatenation or interpolation; use parameterized queries (prepared statements) with placeholders.
quote: SQL Injection is best prevented through the use of [*parameterized queries*](SQL_Injection_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.md).
source: OWASP CS: Query Parameterization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

associative: building SQL string with user input

subject: Session ID Name Fingerprinting
truth: Change the default session ID name of the web development framework to a generic name such as 'id' to prevent technology fingerprinting.
quote: It is recommended to change the default session ID name of the web development framework to a generic name, such as `id`.
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: DTD entity expansion (Billion Laughs / XXE)
truth: Disable DTD processing entirely in XML parsers to prevent entity expansion attacks (Billion Laughs) and external entity injection (XXE).
quote: When an XML parser tries to resolve the external entities included within the following code, it will cause the application to start consuming all of the available memory until the process crashes.
source: OWASP CS: XML Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: permanent admin rights
truth: Stop granting permanent admin rights with periodic reviews; use just-in-time access with automatic expiration and continuous monitoring.
quote: Just-in-time access with automatic expiration and continuous monitoring
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Zero Trust implementation pace
truth: Do not rush Zero Trust implementation in a few months; treat it as a multi-year journey with careful planning and phased implementation.
quote: Moving too fast
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: atoi for port conversion
truth: Stop using atoi for port/network conversions; use istringstream with range checks instead because atoi silently fails on invalid input.
quote: Notice `atoi` and friends are not used because they can silently fail.
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: secure random number generation
truth: Use cryptographically secure PRNGs (CSPRNGs) for security-critical randomness; never use PRNGs like rand(), Math.random(), or Random().
quote: Pseudo-Random Number Generators (PRNG) provide low-quality randomness
source: OWASP CS: Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: hard-coded encryption keys in source code
truth: Do not hard-code keys into the application source code; use secure storage mechanisms like HSMs, key vaults, or OS secure storage APIs.
quote: Do not hard-code keys into the application source code.
source: OWASP CS: Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Kubernetes security context
truth: Set securityContext with readOnlyRootFilesystem and runAsNonRoot on every pod; do not run containers as root with writable filesystems.
quote: any attack that depends on installing software or writing to the file system will be stopped if you specify read-only root file systems
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MCP tool output fed back into LLM context unsanitized
truth: Treat every tool response as untrusted user input and sanitize it before feeding back into the LLM context to prevent prompt injection.
quote: - Treat every tool response as **untrusted user input** — sanitize before feeding back into the LLM context.
source: OWASP CS: MCP (Model Context Protocol) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Shared cache without tenant isolation
truth: Stop using bare cache keys without tenant context; prefix all cache keys with a tenant identifier to prevent cross-tenant data leakage.
quote: Prefix all cache keys with tenant identifier
code_good:
def _build_key(self, tenant_id: str, key: str) -> str:
    """Build tenant-scoped cache key."""
    # Validate key format to prevent injection
    if not key or any(c in key for c in ['{', '}', '\n', '\r']):
        raise ValueError("Invalid cache key format")
    
    # Use hash of tenant_id to prevent key enumeration
    tenant_hash = hashlib.sha256(tenant_id.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
    return f"t:{tenant_hash}:{key}"
source: OWASP CS: Multi-Tenant Application Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MFA factor changes
truth: Require reauthentication with an existing enrolled factor before allowing MFA factor changes; do not rely solely on the active session.
quote: Require reauthentication with an existing enrolled factor before allowing changes
source: OWASP CS: Multifactor Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CSS selectors revealing application features
truth: Stop using descriptive CSS selector names that reveal features or roles; rewrite selectors to be obscure or use build-time obfuscation.
quote: attackers could map these selectors to actual features of a web application
source: OWASP CS: Securing Cascading Style Sheets Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Framing protection against XS-Leaks via element ID focus
truth: Set Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors or X-Frame-Options to prevent your application from being embedded in cross-origin iframes.
quote: Setting up framing protection efficiently blocks the ability to embed your application in a frame on the attacker-controlled origin
source: OWASP CS: Cross-site leaks Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: per-session access granting
truth: Stop granting permanent access; grant access to resources on a per-session basis with short-lived sessions requiring re-authentication.
quote: Don't give permanent access to anything
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: session cookie theft detection false negatives
truth: Do not rely solely on IP-Geo or single-attribute checks to detect cookie theft; they produce both false positives and false negatives.
quote: At the same time, even if the IP-Geo does not change, there is also the possibility that the attacker is attacking from within the same country.
source: OWASP CS: Cookie Theft Mitigation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Naive Double-Submit Cookie pattern
truth: Stop using the Naive Double-Submit Cookie pattern; use the Signed Double-Submit Cookie pattern with session-bound HMAC tokens instead.
quote: The Naive Double-Submit Cookie pattern is bypassable by an attacker who can write cookies on the target domain
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Angular bypassSecurityTrustAs*
truth: Stop using Angular's bypassSecurityTrustAs* functions; they disable Angular's built-in XSS protection and require manual sanitization.
quote: - Angular’s `bypassSecurityTrustAs*` functions
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MAVLink heartbeat messages
truth: Secure heartbeat messages to avoid command injection vulnerabilities; do not send unsigned heartbeat bytes at predictable frequencies.
quote: - You must secure **heartbeat messages** to avoid [command injection vulnerabilities](https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Command_Injection).
source: OWASP CS: Drone Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: logging email addresses and tokens
truth: Never log verification, reset, or authentication tokens or full verification/reset URLs; mask or pseudonymize email addresses in logs.
quote: Never log verification, reset, or authentication tokens or full verification/reset URLs
source: OWASP CS: Email Validation and Verification in Identity Systems Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Java Servlet error page exposes exception details
truth: Stop exposing exception.getMessage() to the client; log the exception server-side and return a generic response without error details.
quote: String errorMessage = exception.getMessage();
source: OWASP CS: Error Handling Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Mounting .npmrc as Docker secret instead of COPY
truth: Mount .npmrc as a Docker build secret using --mount=type=secret instead of copying it into the image, and add .npmrc to .dockerignore.
quote: - The `.npmrc` file is mounted as a secret and is never copied into the Docker image.
source: OWASP CS: Node.js Docker Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: trusting system CA store for mobile pinning
truth: Do not rely solely on the system's built-in CA trust store for pinning; an attacker who can update the trust store can defeat pinning.
quote: an attacker with the access to update a trust store will have the access to change the workings of the mobile application, potentially defeating pinning
source: OWASP CS: Pinning Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SQL injection prevention in Ruby ActiveRecord
truth: Stop interpolating user input into SQL strings; use ActiveRecord's parameterized conditions with ? placeholders or symbol hash syntax.
quote: Parameterized Query Examples
source: OWASP CS: Query Parameterization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XML Signature Wrapping via getElementsByTagName
truth: Never use getElementsByTagName to select security-related XML elements; always use absolute XPath expressions after schema validation.
quote: - Never use `getElementsByTagName` to select security related elements in an XML document without prior validation.
source: OWASP CS: SAML Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Azure Key Vault permissions model
truth: Stop granting permissions at the vault level for separate workloads; do create separate Key Vaults per workload and sensitivity level.
quote: This means secrets for separate workloads and separate sensitivity levels should be in separated Key Vaults accordingly.
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Reflected XSS via user-controlled timer in JavaScript
truth: Stop passing unsanitized user input directly into setTimeout/setInterval; validate and constrain the delay value to a numeric integer.
quote: Reflected XSS in JavaScript
source: OWASP CS: XSS Filter Evasion Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SameSite cookie default Lax in Chromium
truth: Do not assume cookies without SameSite attribute are sent cross-site; Chromium defaults to Lax, which blocks most cross-site requests.
quote: cookies without SameSite attribute set by default are treated as Lax
source: OWASP CS: Cross-site leaks Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Bean Validation @SafeHtml deprecated
truth: Stop using @SafeHtml; it is deprecated and removed. Use a dedicated HTML sanitization library like OWASP Java HTML Sanitizer instead.
quote: Note that `@SafeHtml`, a previously valid constraint, has been deprecated according to the [Hibernate Validator 6.1.0.Final and 6.0.18.Final release blogpost](https://in.relation.to/2019/11/20/hiberna
source: OWASP CS: Bean Validation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Bean Validation cascading with @Valid
truth: Apply @Valid on nested bean fields to trigger cascading validation of the object graph; without it, nested objects are not validated.
quote: To validate that graph in one go, apply cascading validation with [@Valid](https://docs.jboss.org/hibernate/validator/9.0/reference/en-US/html_single/#_cascaded_validation)
source: OWASP CS: Bean Validation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Bean Validation custom error messages
truth: Always specify a message ID in validation annotations and externalize error messages via MessageSource instead of hardcoding strings.
quote: specify a message ID with the validation annotation, so that error messages are customized
source: OWASP CS: Bean Validation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Least privilege for CI/CD pipeline identities
truth: Adopt a deny-by-default mindset and assign only the minimum permissions necessary for each pipeline identity to perform its function.
quote: One should adopt a "deny by default" mindset.
source: OWASP CS: CI/CD Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: safe HTML attributes list
truth: Only use attributes from the safe HTML attributes list for user-controlled values; for others, ensure JavaScript code cannot execute.
quote: **Safe HTML Attributes include:** `align`, `alink`, `alt`, `bgcolor`, `border`, `cellpadding`, `cellspacing`, `class`, `color`, `cols`, `colspan`, `coords`, `dir`, `face`, `height`, `hspace`, `ismap`,
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: IaC immutable infrastructure
truth: Do not mutate running infrastructure; provision a new set of infrastructure from updated specifications and decommission the old set.
quote: If a change to a specification is required, then a whole new set of infrastructure is provisioned based on the updated requirements, and the previous infrastructure is taken out of service as obsolete.
source: OWASP CS: Infrastructure as Code Security Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: JWT token revocation — denylist keyed on raw token or its hash
truth: Key the token denylist on the 'jti' and 'iss' claims, not on a hash of the raw token, because JWT byte representations are malleable.
quote: Do not use the raw JWT or a hash of it as the denylist key
source: OWASP CS: JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MCP server tool definitions after approval
truth: Stop allowing MCP servers to change tool definitions after user approval; a rug pull attack swaps a trusted tool for a malicious one.
quote: A server changes its tool definitions after initial user approval, turning a trusted tool malicious
source: OWASP CS: MCP (Model Context Protocol) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Mass Assignment via automatic parameter binding
truth: Stop binding HTTP request parameters directly to domain objects; use a Data Transfer Object (DTO) that only includes editable fields.
quote: avoid binding input directly to domain objects
source: OWASP CS: Mass Assignment Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SAML schema validation with external schemas
truth: Always use local, trusted copies of schemas for XML validation; never allow automatic download of schemas from third-party locations.
quote: - Never allow automatic download of schemas from third party locations.
source: OWASP CS: SAML Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SAML signature algorithm SHA-1 rejection
truth: Explicitly verify the signature algorithm is at least RSA-SHA-256 and reject SHA-1-based algorithms including rsa-sha1 and hmac-sha1.
quote: Explicitly verify the signature algorithm is at least RSA-SHA-256 (or stronger). Reject SHA-1-based algorithms
source: OWASP CS: SAML Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MLFlow authentication
truth: Enable authentication and access controls on MLFlow and similar model registries to prevent exposure of all models and training logs.
quote: Open MLFlow Instance: No authentication on MLFlow or similar tool exposes all models and training logs.
source: OWASP CS: Secure AI/ML Model Ops Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Treating output from one agent as trusted input to another agent
truth: Treat output from one agent as untrusted input when passed to another agent — prompt injection can propagate across agent boundaries.
quote: Treat output from one agent as untrusted input when passed to another agent
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AI code generation with outdated dependencies
truth: Verify that AI-suggested dependency versions actually exist and are not deprecated, unmaintained, or known-vulnerable (LLM package/version hallucination — "slopsquatting" — is a real risk); pin dependencies to a specific, tested version via a lock file rather than accepting an AI-suggested version blindly, and update deliberately — driven by disclosed CVEs/patches and reviewed before merge — rather than automatically chasing the newest release on every cycle.
quote: Outdated Dependencies
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA
_audit_fix: MISLEADING: The quote is only the section heading 'Outdated Dependencies' with no supporting text, so it doesn't substantiate the specific claim; also 'always pin to the ne

subject: Session ID Content Must Be Meaningless
truth: The session ID value must be meaningless and must never include sensitive information or PII; store associated data server-side only.
quote: The session ID content (or value) must be meaningless to prevent information disclosure attacks
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Unvalidated forward via request dispatcher with user input
truth: Stop forwarding to user-supplied paths via getRequestDispatcher; validate the forward target against a whitelist of authorized pages.
quote: The following code is a Java servlet that will receive a `GET` request with a URL parameter named `fwd` in the request to forward to the address specified in the URL parameter.
source: OWASP CS: Unvalidated Redirects and Forwards Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Client-side security logic
truth: Never rely on client-side logic for security or business rules — the user controls the client and can bypass any client-side checks.
quote: Never rely on client logic for security
source: OWASP CS: AJAX Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Treating extension message senders as trusted by default
truth: Treat all incoming messages as untrusted input and validate sender origin even when the message comes from your own content scripts.
quote: Treat all incoming messages as untrusted input.
source: OWASP CS: Browser Extension Security Vulnerabilities Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: GCC overflow removal
truth: Always add -fno-strict-overflow and -fwrapv to prevent the compiler from removing overflow or wrap checks that your code depends on.
quote: The flags ensure the compiler does not remove statements that result in overflow or wrap
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Multi-step login processes enabling user enumeration
truth: Ensure multi-step login processes do not facilitate user enumeration, which can make credential stuffing attacks harder to identify.
quote: Multi-step login processes, however, should be mindful that they do not facilitate [user enumeration](Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.md).
source: OWASP CS: Credential Stuffing Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: window/document named property access for script src
truth: Stop reading script source URLs from window or document named properties; use a hardcoded or validated configuration object instead.
quote: When accessing an attribute of `window` and `document` objects, named HTML element references come before lookups of built-in APIs and other attributes on `window` and `document` that developers have
source: OWASP CS: DOM Clobbering Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CAPTCHA for DoS defense
truth: Do not rely on CAPTCHAs or input puzzles to defend against DoS attacks; they only prevent functionality abuse like mailbox flooding.
quote: Puzzles serve a purpose against functionality abuse but this kind of technology will not help defend against DoS attacks.
source: OWASP CS: Denial of Service Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Security questions as sole password reset mechanism
truth: Do not use security questions as the sole mechanism for password reset; answers are frequently guessable or obtainable by attackers.
quote: Security questions should not be used as the sole mechanism for resetting passwords due to their answers frequently being easily guessable or obtainable by attackers.
source: OWASP CS: Forgot Password Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: self-hosted runners on public repositories
truth: Never use self-hosted runners with public repositories; anyone who can fork and open a pull request can execute code on your runner.
quote: never use self-hosted runners with public repositories, as anyone who can fork the repository and open a pull request can potentially execute code on your runner
source: OWASP CS: GitHub Actions Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: application code reading cryptographic keys
truth: Stop having standard application-level code read or use cryptographic keys; all key operations must be done inside the sealed vault.
quote: Ensure that standard application level code never reads or uses cryptographic keys in any way and use key management libraries.
source: OWASP CS: Key Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Shell-form CMD signal forwarding
truth: Stop using shell-form CMD (string syntax) in Dockerfiles for Node.js; it wraps the process in a shell that does not forward signals.
quote: the shell may not properly forward signals to your process
source: OWASP CS: Node.js Docker Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SAML Unsolicited Response (IdP-Initiated SSO)
truth: Stop using IdP-initiated SSO without CSRF protection; prefer SP-initiated SSO or apply replay detection and RelayState allowlisting.
quote: Unsolicited Response is inherently less secure by design due to the lack of **login [CSRF](Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.md#possible-csrf-vulnerabilities-in-login-forms)** protecti
source: OWASP CS: SAML Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: multiple cookie verification
truth: Verify all cookies and enforce relationships between them before allowing access to the user session when multiple cookies are used.
quote: the web application must verify all cookies (and enforce relationships between them) before allowing access to the user session
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: unpinned third-party dependencies
truth: Stop using unpinned version ranges for third-party dependencies; pin to exact versions or hashes to prevent supply chain compromise.
quote: using a vulnerable or compromised dependency
source: OWASP CS: Software Supply Chain Security
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Diffie-Hellman parameter generation
truth: Do not set custom Diffie-Hellman parameters for TLS 1.2 or earlier; use the supported_groups extension with standard groups instead.
quote: For TLS 1.2 or earlier versions it is recommended not to set Diffie-Hellman parameters.
source: OWASP CS: Transport Layer Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: session hijacking response
truth: When session hijacking is suspected, re-authenticate the user or present a CAPTCHA rather than blindly trusting the session cookie.
quote: If there is a possibility that a session has been hijacked, the most reliable verification method is to re-authenticate.
source: OWASP CS: Cookie Theft Mitigation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Secrets in source-controlled config files
truth: Do not store secrets in source-controlled config files; use User Secrets for development and a managed secret store for production.
quote: - **Do not store secrets in source-controlled config files (`web.config`, `appsettings.json`).** Keep secrets out of the configuration file entirely.
source: OWASP CS: DotNet Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: cryptographic key storage in plaintext
truth: Stop storing keys in plaintext format; always store keys in a cryptographic vault such as an HSM or isolated cryptographic service.
quote: Keys should never be stored in plaintext format.
source: OWASP CS: Key Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: key compromise recovery plan
truth: Stop operating without a documented compromise-recovery plan; a compromise-recovery plan shall be documented and easily accessible.
quote: A compromise-recovery plan shall be documented and easily accessible.
source: OWASP CS: Key Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: kernel module auto-loading
truth: Prevent Linux from automatically loading unwanted kernel modules by blacklisting them in /etc/modprobe.d/ to reduce attack surface.
quote: even unprivileged processes can cause certain network-protocol-related kernel modules to be loaded
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: allow-listing interception proxies
truth: Do not offer to allow-list an interception proxy; add its public key to your pinset only after being instructed by Risk Acceptance.
quote: In this case, **do not** offer to allow-list the interception proxy since it defeats your security goals.
source: OWASP CS: Pinning Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Raw user input to vector similarity search
truth: Normalize and inspect queries for abuse patterns before retrieval; do not pass raw user input directly to vector similarity search.
quote: Pass raw user input directly to the vector similarity search without inspection and normalization.
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: REST API session state
truth: Stop passing session state from client to backend; REST APIs must be stateless and should not rely on client-carried session state.
quote: Passing state from client to backend, while making the service technically stateless, is an anti-pattern that should also be avoided as it is prone to replay and impersonation attacks.
source: OWASP CS: REST Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: transaction authorization brute-force prevention
truth: After a set number of failed authorization attempts, restart the entire transaction authorization process to prevent brute-forcing.
quote: After a set number of failed authorization attempts, the entire transaction authorization process should be restarted
source: OWASP CS: Transaction Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Permanent admin rights removal
truth: Remove permanent admin rights and switch to temporary, just-in-time privileged access management (PAM) for administrative accounts.
quote: Remove permanent admin rights and switch to temporary access.
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Email addresses as usernames enabling credential stuffing
truth: Stop using email addresses as usernames by default; require users to create their own username to make credential stuffing harder.
quote: A significant number of websites use the email address as the username, and as most users will have a single email address they use for all their accounts, this makes the combination of an email address and password very effective for credential stuffing attacks.
source: OWASP CS: Credential Stuffing Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: File upload size limits
truth: Limit file upload size and extensions to prevent DoS on file storage and processing functions like image resizing or PDF creation.
quote: Limit file upload size and extensions
source: OWASP CS: Denial of Service Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: GraphQL missing depth and amount limiting
truth: Add depth limiting and amount limiting to incoming GraphQL queries to prevent DoS attacks via deeply nested or high-count queries.
quote: By default these can both be unlimited which may lead to a DoS
source: OWASP CS: GraphQL Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LLM output rendering sanitization
truth: Deploy HTML/Markdown sanitization for output rendering to prevent remote injection vectors from executing in downstream consumers.
quote: Deploy HTML/Markdown sanitization for output rendering
source: OWASP CS: LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: sensitive file deletion logging
truth: Do not immediately delete sensitive files; mark them for deletion and maintain an archive according to legal/privacy requirements.
quote: The file should not be immediately deleted but marked for deletion and an archive of the file should be maintained according to legal/privacy requirements.
source: OWASP CS: Application Logging Vocabulary Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: interservice network access between different information systems
truth: Prohibit access between FRONTEND and MIDDLEWARE segments of different information systems; do not allow cross-system network hops.
quote: Access between FRONTEND and MIDDLEWARE segments of different information systems is prohibited
source: OWASP CS: Network segmentation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: password storage in plaintext
truth: Stop storing passwords in plaintext; always hash passwords using strong, slow hashing algorithms like Argon2id, bcrypt, or PBKDF2.
quote: Passwords should never be stored in plain text.
source: OWASP CS: Password Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Custom authentication tokens bypass standard session tracking tools
truth: Reverse engineer custom authentication tokens and emulate them during fuzzing, as standard tools cannot track REST login sessions.
quote: Custom authentication mechanisms require reverse engineering and make popular tools not useful as they cannot track a login session.
source: OWASP CS: REST Assessment Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XSS via link_to href with javascript: protocol
truth: Stop trusting user-supplied URLs in link_to; newer Rails versions escape javascript: links, but validate and sanitize href values.
quote: An often overlooked XSS attack vector for older versions of rails is the `href` value of a link
source: OWASP CS: Ruby on Rails Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: storing encryption keys next to secrets
truth: Never store encryption keys next to the secrets they encrypt unless those keys are themselves encrypted using envelope encryption.
quote: You should not store keys next to the secrets they encrypt, except if those keys are encrypted themselves
source: OWASP CS: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SSRF URL input validation
truth: Do not accept complete URLs from the user; only accept a valid IP address or domain name if network-related information is needed.
quote: Do not accept complete URLs from the user because URL are difficult to validate and the parser can be abused
source: OWASP CS: Server-Side Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Session ID Entropy
truth: Session identifiers must have at least 64 bits of entropy and be generated using a CSPRNG to prevent brute-force guessing attacks.
quote: Session identifiers must have at least `64 bits` of entropy to prevent brute-force session guessing attacks.
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: innerHTML with untrusted data
truth: Never use innerHTML with untrusted data; use textContent for text-only updates or sanitize with DOMPurify before using innerHTML.
quote: never use innerHTML with untrusted data
code_bad: document.getElementById('content').innerHTML = data; // DANGER! The server may have returned a payload that executes scripts, for example: .
source: OWASP CS: AJAX Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: X-Frame-Options vs CSP frame-ancestors priority
truth: Set both the X-Frame-Options and CSP frame-ancestors headers together for clickjacking defense, not one or the other. X-Frame-Options is formally obsolete but costs nothing to send; modern CSP2+ browsers will use frame-ancestors and ignore X-Frame-Options when both are present, but older/legacy browsers that don't support frame-ancestors fall back to honoring X-Frame-Options. Omitting X-Frame-Options in favor of CSP alone leaves those older browsers with no clickjacking protection.
quote: older browser versions (e.g., Chrome 40 & Firefox 35) ignored this requirement and followed the X-Frame-Options header instead
source: OWASP CS: Clickjacking Defense Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA
_audit_fix: MISLEADING: The directive contradicts its own cited reasoning: since older browsers (Chrome 40, Firefox 35) ignore CSP frame-ancestors and honor X-Frame-Options instead, te

subject: pull_request_target trigger
truth: Never use pull_request_target trigger with checkout and execution of untrusted code; use pull_request with labeled event instead.
quote: > Never check out (via `actions/checkout` or GitHub CLI) and run untrusted code in this context.
source: OWASP CS: GitHub Actions Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: container image vulnerability scanning
truth: Integrate a CI pipeline that scans images for vulnerabilities and fails the build if issues are found before pushing to registry.
quote: After an image is built, it should be scanned for security vulnerabilities. Only if no issues are found, then the image would be pushed to a private registry then deployed to production.
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: client authentication
truth: Use asymmetric (public-key based) methods for client authentication such as mTLS or private_key_jwt instead of symmetric secrets.
quote: It is recommended to use asymmetric (public-key based) methods for client authentication such as mTLS or "private_key_jwt" (OpenID Connect).
source: OWASP CS: OAuth 2.0 Protocol Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Custom SSL pinning implementation from scratch
truth: Do not implement pinning validation from scratch; use platform-native declarative configuration or a well-tested library instead.
quote: implementing pinning validation from scratch should be avoided, as implementation mistakes are extremely likely and usually lead to severe vulnerabilities
source: OWASP CS: Pinning Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: runtime isolation for untrusted model workloads
truth: Run untrusted model evaluation, fine-tuning, and conversion jobs in sandboxes or isolated workers with restricted network egress.
quote: Run untrusted model evaluation, fine-tuning, and conversion jobs in sandboxes or isolated workers with restricted network egress.
source: OWASP CS: Secure AI/ML Model Ops Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: AI-generated code dependencies
truth: Do not trust AI-generated dependency names; verify each package exists in the official registry before adding it to your project.
quote: Hallucinated Dependencies
source: OWASP CS: Secure Coding with AI Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Hashing vs Encryption for Passwords
truth: Stop encrypting passwords; hash them with a salt using Argon2 or bcrypt, because hashing is one-way and encryption is reversible.
quote: Used for password storage (with salt) and verifying file integrity.
source: OWASP CS: Security Terminology Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: unique authorization credentials per transaction
truth: Stop reusing the same authorization credentials across transactions; each transaction must be authorized with unique credentials.
quote: Each transaction should be authorized using unique authorization credentials
source: OWASP CS: Transaction Authorization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: web service resource limits
truth: Limit CPU cycles, memory, open files, network connections, and started processes for web services to prevent resource exhaustion.
quote: Limit the amount of CPU cycles the web service can use
source: OWASP CS: Web Service Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Zero Trust network security mindset
truth: Stop relying only on network security; verify identity and device health for every access request regardless of network location.
quote: Relying only on network security
source: OWASP CS: Zero Trust Architecture Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Browser extension code injection via dynamic script loading
truth: Stop dynamically loading scripts from untrusted sources; use CSP to restrict script sources and prefer extension messaging APIs.
quote: dynamically loads scripts from an untrusted source can be exploited
source: OWASP CS: Browser Extension Security Vulnerabilities Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: ALLOW-FROM directive
truth: Stop using the obsolete ALLOW-FROM directive; it no longer works in modern browsers and fails open with no clickjacking defense.
quote: This is an obsolete directive that no longer works in modern browsers
source: OWASP CS: Clickjacking Defense Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CSRF protection via custom request headers
truth: Stop relying on tokens for AJAX/API endpoints; use a custom request header instead, which browsers will preflight automatically.
quote: A user-friendly defense that is particularly well suited for AJAX or API endpoints is the use of a **custom request header**.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: JavaScript context variable placement
truth: Stop placing variables in arbitrary JavaScript contexts; only place them inside quoted data values and encode using \xHH format.
quote: However, the only ‘safe’ location for placing variables in JavaScript is inside a “quoted data value”.
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: database credentials in source code
truth: Stop storing database credentials in application source code; store them in a protected configuration file outside the web root.
quote: Database credentials should never be stored in the application source code
source: OWASP CS: Database Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: WCF RESTful service HTTP method
truth: Only use HTTP POST to pass requests in RESTful WCF services with TLS enabled; do not use HTTP GET which exposes data in the URL.
quote: - Keep in mind that the only safe way to pass a request in RESTful services is via `HTTP POST`, with TLS enabled.
source: OWASP CS: DotNet Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Laravel rate limiting without per-user/per-IP scoping
truth: Always scope rate limits with ->by() to tie limits to a user ID or IP, otherwise the limit applies globally across all requests.
quote: Limit::perMinute(5)->by($request->user()?->id ?: $request->ip())
source: OWASP CS: Laravel Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: MCP tool URL fetching without allowlist validation
truth: Never fetch arbitrary URLs provided by the LLM without strict allowlist validation to prevent SSRF attacks on internal services.
quote: Never fetch arbitrary URLs provided by the LLM without strict allowlist validation
source: OWASP CS: MCP (Model Context Protocol) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: RAG document storage without integrity verification
truth: Store document hashes at ingestion and verify them before retrieval; without integrity verification you cannot detect tampering.
quote: - Store documents without integrity verification -- you will have no way to detect tampering later.
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: RAG access control failure
truth: When an access control check fails, return nothing — not a filtered subset — because a failed check may indicate a system error.
quote: A failed access control check may indicate a system error, not a clean result.
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SQL injection in WordPress Shopping Cart reqID parameter
truth: Stop passing unsanitized user input from the reqID parameter into SQL queries; apply input validation and parameterized queries.
quote: The issue is due to the `/wp-content/plugins/levelfourstorefront/scripts/administration/exportsubscribers.php` script not properly sanitizing user-supplied input to the `reqID` parameter.
source: OWASP CS: Virtual Patching Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: postMessage targetOrigin wildcard
truth: Always specify the exact targetOrigin in postMessage; never use the wildcard '*' which allows any origin to receive the message.
quote: Passing to the `targetOrigin` wildcard `*` causes any origin to receive the message.
source: OWASP CS: Cross-site leaks Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Automake does not support build configurations
truth: Do not rely on Automake to support separate debug/release configurations; use a build system that tracks configuration changes.
quote: Automake does not support the concept of configurations
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: NDEBUG removing program diagnostics in release builds
truth: Stop relying on assert() and abort() in production code; define NDEBUG in release builds and use proper error handling instead.
quote: `NDEBUG` removes "program diagnostics" and has undesirable behavior and side effects which are discussed below in more detail.
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: PIE linking
truth: Stop linking position-dependent executables; add -fPIE at compile time and -pie at link time to enable ASLR for the executable.
quote:
MY_CC_FLAGS += -fPIE
    MY_LD_FLAGS += -pie
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Lit unsafeHTML
truth: Stop using Lit's unsafeHTML function; it disables Lit's templating protections and can lead to XSS if content is not sanitized.
quote: Lit's `unsafeHTML` function
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Account lockout on forgotten password
truth: Do not lock out accounts in response to a forgotten password attack, as this enables denial-of-service against known usernames.
quote: Accounts should not be locked out in response to a forgotten password attack, as this can be used to deny access to users with known usernames.
source: OWASP CS: Forgot Password Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Java SQL query with string concatenation vs PreparedStatement
truth: Stop building SQL queries with string concatenation; use PreparedStatement with parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection.
quote: Prepared statements ensure that an attacker is not able to change the intent of a query, even if SQL commands are inserted by an attacker.
source: OWASP CS: Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Input validation vs output encoding for XSS
truth: Do not rely on input validation as the primary defense against Cross-Site Scripting; use context-aware output encoding instead.
quote: It's also free-form text input that highlights the importance of proper context-aware output encoding and quite clearly demonstrates that input validation is **not** the primary safeguards against Cro
source: OWASP CS: Input Validation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: unauthorized container images
truth: Only allow container images that adhere to organizational policy and are from authorized registries to run in your environment.
quote: Make sure that only images adhering to the organization’s policy are allowed to run, or else the organization is open to risk of running vulnerable or even malicious containers.
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Kubernetes resource limits
truth: Define resource quotas for containers to prevent DoS and noisy neighbor scenarios; do not deploy with unbounded CPU and memory.
quote: all resources in a Kubernetes cluster are created with unbounded CPU limits and memory requests/limits by default
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: logging SQL injection payloads
truth: Do not log the full SQL injection payload; log only the detection rule ID/category and parameter name to prevent log injection.
quote: Logging the payload is dangerous and may result in log injection. Prefer recording a detection rule ID / category and the parameter name over logging the full payload.
source: OWASP CS: Application Logging Vocabulary Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: insecure randomness in file upload filename generation
truth: Stop using Math.random() for filename generation in file uploads; use crypto.randomBytes(16) as the library's internal default.
quote: A file upload library's README generates filenames with `Math.random()` while the library's own default uses `crypto.randomBytes(16)`.
source: OWASP CS: NPM Security best practices
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Node.js uncaughtException resuming the process
truth: Do not resume the application after an uncaughtException; clean up resources and call process.exit() to avoid an unknown state.
quote: Resuming the application is strongly discouraged as the application will be in an unknown state
source: OWASP CS: NodeJS Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: OAuth 2.0 Resource Owner Password Credentials Grant
truth: Stop using the Resource Owner Password Credentials Grant; it insecurely exposes the resource owner's credentials to the client.
quote: The Resource Owner password credentials grant is not used. This grant type insecurely exposes the credentials of the Resource Owner to the client
source: OWASP CS: OAuth 2.0 Protocol Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: System prompt positioning before retrieved content
truth: Reinforce system instructions after retrieved content; do not rely solely on system prompt positioning before retrieved chunks.
quote: Reinforce system instructions after retrieved content. Positioning should be tested per model, as attention patterns vary.
source: OWASP CS: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XSS via raw/html_safe output in ERB templates
truth: Stop using raw, html_safe, or <%== to output user content in views; let Rails auto-escape or use a sanitizer with an allowlist.
quote: Unfortunately, any field that uses `raw`, `html_safe` or similar like this will be a potential XSS target.
source: OWASP CS: Ruby on Rails Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: WebSocket origin validation
truth: Validate the Origin header on the server side during the WebSocket handshake to prevent Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking (CSWSH).
quote: [Insufficient origin validation](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-f53g-frr2-jhpf) allowed full account takeover via hijacked WebSocket connections
source: OWASP CS: WebSocket Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: XSS via unvalidated redirect header in PHP
truth: Stop passing unsanitized user input directly into the Location or Refresh header; validate against a whitelist of allowed URLs.
quote: XSS via request Redirection
source: OWASP CS: XSS Filter Evasion Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: gRPC rate limiting without cleanup
truth: Implement periodic cleanup of stale rate limiter entries to prevent memory exhaustion from unbounded growth of the limiter map.
quote: Cleanup old limiters periodically
source: OWASP CS: gRPC Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: password manager compatibility
truth: Use standard HTML forms with appropriate type attributes for username and password input; do not use plugin-based login pages.
quote: - Use standard HTML forms for username and password input with appropriate `type` attributes.
source: OWASP CS: Authentication Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: jQuery $.ajaxSetup CSRF token missing crossDomain check
truth: When using jQuery $.ajaxSetup for CSRF tokens, always check !settings.crossDomain to avoid sending tokens to external origins.
quote: if (!csrfSafeMethod(settings.type) && !settings.crossDomain)
source: OWASP CS: Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: UUID version selection
truth: Use only version 4 (random) UUIDs for security purposes; never use version 1 UUIDs which contain timestamps and MAC addresses.
quote: Specifically, version 1 UUIDs are comprised of a high precision timestamp and the MAC address of the system that generated them, so are **not random** (although they may be hard to guess, given the ti
source: OWASP CS: Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Spring @ExceptionHandler returning exception details
truth: Stop leaking exception details in error responses; use ProblemDetail with a generic message and log the exception server-side.
quote: return ProblemDetail.forStatusAndDetail(HttpStatus.INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR, "An error occur, please retry");
source: OWASP CS: Error Handling Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Closed source applications require virtual patching for injection
truth: When source code is unavailable or uneconomical to fix, apply virtual patching instead of leaving injection flaws unaddressed.
quote: if the source code is not available or it is simply uneconomical to fix legacy software only virtual patching makes sense.
source: OWASP CS: Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Kubelet unauthenticated access
truth: Enable Kubelet authentication and authorization; by default Kubelets allow unauthenticated access to powerful HTTPS endpoints.
quote: By default Kubelets allow unauthenticated access to this API
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: authorization failures in audit logs
truth: Monitor audit logs for 'Forbidden' status messages — authorization failures can indicate an attacker using stolen credentials.
quote: Ensure logs that are monitoring for anomalous or unwanted API calls, especially any authorization failures (these log entries will have a status message “Forbidden”).
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: LDAP bind authentication
truth: Prevent LDAP injection primarily by escaping/encoding special characters in any user input used to build LDAP distinguished names or search filters (DN specials: \ # + < > , ; " = and leading/trailing spaces; filter specials: * ( ) \ NUL), or by using a framework/parameterized-filter API that does this encoding automatically (e.g. filter placeholders with input passed as separate parameters, not concatenated). Enabling authenticated bind (and disabling anonymous/unauthenticated bind) is an additional hardening measure — not a substitute for input escaping — since anonymous or unauthenticated bind can itself be used to bypass the authentication check.
quote: attackers would not be able to perform LDAP injection attacks because of verification and authorization checks
source: OWASP CS: LDAP Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA
_audit_fix: MISLEADING: This verbatim-matches one narrow line from the OWASP cheat sheet, but presented alone it conflates authentication mode with injection prevention — the cheat she

subject: Node.js Docker CMD patterns
truth: Stop using npm/yarn/scripts as CMD; invoke node directly with an init system like dumb-init to handle PID 1 signal forwarding.
quote: Nothing happened, right? That’s because the npm client doesn’t forward any signals to the node process that it spawned.
source: OWASP CS: Node.js Docker Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: password salting
truth: Stop manually generating salts; use modern password hashing libraries that automatically generate and manage salts internally.
quote: most widely used implementations and libraries automatically generate and manage salts internally, so application developers typically do not need to handle salt generation manually when using these libraries correctly.
source: OWASP CS: Password Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: work factor selection
truth: Do not pick a work factor without testing on your specific server hardware; calculate hashes should take less than one second.
quote: Determining the optimal work factor will require experimentation on the specific server(s) used by the application. As a general rule, calculating a hash should take less than one second.
source: OWASP CS: Password Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: SQL injection via string interpolation in ActiveRecord where clause
truth: Stop building SQL queries with string interpolation; use parameterized queries with '?' placeholders and sanitize LIKE inputs.
quote: The statement is injectable because the name parameter is not escaped.
source: OWASP CS: Ruby on Rails Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Panic mode discoverability
truth: Do not make panic modes easily discoverable; an adversary inside a panic mode must not be able to find out it is a panic mode.
quote: An important note about panic modes is that they must not be easily discoverable, if at all.
source: OWASP CS: User Privacy Protection Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: WebSocket Origin header validation
truth: Validate the Origin header on every WebSocket handshake using an explicit allowlist to prevent Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking.
quote: Validate the `Origin` header on every handshake.
source: OWASP CS: WebSocket Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Makefile rebuild with changed CFLAGS
truth: Stop relying on Make to detect changed CFLAGS; force a clean rebuild when switching between debug and release configurations.
quote: When you want the release build, Make will do nothing because it considers everything up to date despite the fact `CFLAGS` and `CXXFLAGS` have changed.
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: thick client direct database access
truth: Never connect directly from a thick client to the backend database; always route through an API that enforces access control.
quote: Direct connections should **never** be made from a thick client to the backend database.
source: OWASP CS: Database Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: GraphQL batching attack via object enumeration
truth: Prevent batching for sensitive objects (usernames, emails, passwords, OTPs, session tokens) to block brute-force enumeration.
quote: prevent batching for sensitive objects that you don't want to be brute forced
source: OWASP CS: GraphQL Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Spring MVC block-listing approach for mass assignment
truth: Prefer allow-listing with setAllowedFields() over block-listing with setDisallowedFields() to avoid missing dangerous fields.
quote: binder.setDisallowedFields(["isAdmin"]);
source: OWASP CS: Mass Assignment Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: PKCE code challenge method
truth: Use PKCE code challenge methods that do not expose the PKCE verifier in the authorization request; use S256 instead of plain.
quote: Clients should use PKCE code challenge methods that do not expose the PKCE verifier in the authorization request. Otherwise, attackers who can read the authorization request can break the security provided by the PKCE.
source: OWASP CS: OAuth 2.0 Protocol Cheatsheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: PHP escapeshellcmd vs escapeshellarg for user input
truth: Stop using escapeshellcmd() when user input is a single argument; use escapeshellarg() instead to prevent argument injection.
quote: It is always preferable to use `escapeshellarg()` rather than `escapeshellcmd()` when dealing with user input.
source: OWASP CS: OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: password hashing without a unique salt
truth: Stop hashing passwords without a unique salt; add a unique salt to each password to prevent precomputed lookup table attacks.
quote: A unique salt must be added to each password to prevent attackers from using precomputed lookup tables like rainbow tables.
source: OWASP CS: Password Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Rust SQLx string interpolation in queries
truth: Stop formatting SQL strings with user input; use SQLx's query macros with ? placeholders or bind() to pass parameters safely.
quote: Using Rust with SQLx
source: OWASP CS: Query Parameterization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

associative: format!("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = {}", username)

subject: session ID regeneration after privilege change
truth: Regenerate the session ID after any privilege level change (login, password change, role switch) to prevent session fixation.
quote: The session ID must be renewed or regenerated by the web application after any privilege level change
source: OWASP CS: Session Management Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: exec() with unsanitized user input
truth: Stop using exec() with unsanitized user input; use native PHP functions like unlink() or Symfony Filesystem remove() instead.
quote: no validation of the user's input
source: OWASP CS: Symfony Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: javascript: and data: URLs in React
truth: Stop assuming React handles javascript: or data: URLs safely; React cannot handle these URLs without specialized validation.
quote: React cannot handle `javascript:` or `data:` URLs without specialized validation
source: OWASP CS: Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: independent key derivation for multiple keys
truth: When using multiple keys (e.g., data-encrypting and key-encrypting keys), generate them fully independently from each other.
quote: they should be fully independent from each other
source: OWASP CS: Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: JavaScript encoding in execution contexts
truth: JavaScript encoding does not stop XSS attacks within an execution context; JavaScript-encoded strings still execute as code.
quote: JavaScript encoding does not mitigate against DOM based XSS
source: OWASP CS: DOM based XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: ARP poisoning prevention
truth: Do not rely on dynamic ARP alone; use packet filtering to inspect and block offending ARP packets, or use static ARP tables.
quote: Packet filtering technology can be used to inspect packets in transit to identify and block offending ARP packets
source: OWASP CS: Denial of Service Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Insecure Direct Object Reference in .NET
truth: Stop returning a resource by ID without checking the user's ownership; add an authorization check before returning the view.
quote: When you have a resource (object) which can be accessed by a reference (in the sample below this is the `id`), you need to ensure that the user is intended to have access to that resource.
source: OWASP CS: DotNet Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: File upload extension whitelisting
truth: List allowed extensions and only allow safe and critical extensions for business functionality; do not rely on blacklisting.
quote: List allowed extensions. Only allow safe and critical extensions for business functionality
source: OWASP CS: File Upload Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: HTML5 Application Cache (manifest) is deprecated
truth: Stop using the HTML5 Application Cache (<html manifest> and .appcache files); migrate to Service Workers with the Cache API.
quote: - The HTML5 Application Cache (`<html manifest="...">` and `.appcache` files) has been **removed from all major browsers** (Firefox 85, Chrome 93).
source: OWASP CS: HTML5 Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: JWT algorithm validation — none algorithm
truth: Explicitly request the expected algorithm during JWT verification; do not rely on the library's default algorithm selection.
quote: explicitly request that the expected algorithm was used
source: OWASP CS: JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: audit logging disabled by default
truth: Enable audit logging in kube-apiserver using --audit-policy-file; without it no events are logged and attacks go undetected.
quote: If the flag is omitted, no events are logged
source: OWASP CS: Kubernetes Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Raw Query SQL Injection
truth: Stop concatenating user input into raw SQL strings; always use SQL bindings (positional ? or named :param) for request data.
quote: Always remember to use SQL bindings for request data
source: OWASP CS: Laravel Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: npm token creation
truth: Stop using long-lived npm tokens for CI/CD; use trusted publishing with OIDC for short-lived, workflow-specific credentials.
quote: Traditional npm publishing relies on long-lived tokens that can be compromised or accidentally exposed.
source: OWASP CS: NPM Security best practices
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: URL segments mistaken for static paths when they are parameters
truth: Treat repeating or highly varying URL segments as potential parameters, not static paths, to avoid missing injection points.
quote: Look for highly varying URL segments - a single URL segment that has many values may be parameter and not a physical directory.
source: OWASP CS: REST Assessment Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: JWT algorithm selection from header
truth: Stop trusting the JWT header's alg field to select the verification algorithm; hard-code the expected algorithm server-side.
quote: A relying party must verify the integrity of the JWT based on its own configuration or hard-coded logic. It must not rely on the information of the JWT header to select the verification algorithm.
source: OWASP CS: REST Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: HTTP method allowlisting
truth: Apply an allowlist of permitted HTTP methods and reject all others with 405 Method Not Allowed; do not rely on blacklisting.
quote: - Apply an allowlist of permitted HTTP Methods e.g.
source: OWASP CS: REST Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: out-of-order API execution
truth: Enforce workflow state validation on the server side for every request; do not rely on frontend logic to enforce sequencing.
quote: Enforce workflow state validation on the server side for every request
source: OWASP CS: REST Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: WAF bypass using data: URI scheme in redirect
truth: Stop allowing data: URIs in redirect parameters; validate redirect targets against a strict allowlist of known-safe origins.
quote: This request will pass through the WAF and an XSS attack will be conducted in certain browsers
source: OWASP CS: XSS Filter Evasion Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Undefining security macros
truth: Do not undefine _FORTIFY_SOURCE on Linux or define _CRT_SECURE_NO_WARNINGS on Windows, as these disable security hardening.
quote: For example, `-U_FORTIFY_SOURCES` on Linux and `_CRT_SECURE_NO_WARNINGS=1`, `_SCL_SECURE_NO_WARNINGS`, `_ATL_SECURE_NO_WARNINGS` or `STRSAFE_NO_DEPRECATE` on Windows.
source: OWASP CS: C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: CSP allowlist-based policies
truth: Prefer a strict CSP over granular allowlist-based CSPs, which are more likely to be bypassed and harder to deploy securely.
quote: However, current leading practice is to create a "Strict" CSP which is much easier to deploy and more secure as it is less likely to be bypassed.
source: OWASP CS: Content Security Policy Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: server-side validation
truth: Implement input validation on the server-side, not only client-side; client-side JavaScript validation can be circumvented.
quote: Input validation **must** be implemented on the server-side before any data is processed by an application’s functions, as any JavaScript-based input validation performed on the client-side can be cir
source: OWASP CS: Input Validation Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: IDOR via filename reference in URL
truth: Stop treating filenames in URLs as safe object references; enforce access control checks for every document or file access.
quote: the filename acts as the object reference. If an attacker modifies the filename to another valid document
source: OWASP CS: Insecure Direct Object Reference Prevention Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: logging CSP violation URLs with query strings
truth: Strip the query string and fragment from CSP violation URL fields before logging to avoid exposing session or reset tokens.
quote: routinely contain full URLs whose query strings carry session or reset tokens. Strip the query string/fragment before logging
source: OWASP CS: Application Logging Vocabulary Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Accepting raw JSON queries from clients
truth: Do not accept raw JSON fragments from the client to execute as queries; reject operator injection by disallowing $ in keys.
quote: - Do **not** accept raw JSON fragments from the client to execute as queries.

subject: libxml2 on iOS (up through iOS6)

Defaults have varied across API layers and versions over time (e.g., NSXMLParser's own external-entity resolution remained on by default independent of libxml2's version-level fix, until patched in iOS 8.3/CVE-2015-1092), so version-checking alone is not a reliable safety signal — verify and lock down entity/DTD handling per-parser and per-configuration.
quote: the version of libxml2 provided up through iOS6 is prior to version 2.9 of libxml2 (which protects against XXE by default)

subject: SQLite secure deletion
truth: Define SQLITE_SECURE_DELETE (not SQLITE_HAS_CODEC) to enable secure deletion that zeroizes memory on destruction — this pragma-default causes deleted content to be overwritten with zeros, which is required for FIPS 140-2 Level 1 (per OWASP's C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet, always define it in US Federal builds). SQLITE_HAS_CODEC is a separate, unrelated (and since SQLite 3.32.0, removed/unsupported) macro that only enables the encryption-codec interface used by extensions like SQLCipher/SEE.
quote: SQLite secure deletion zeroizes memory on destruction. Define as required, and always define in US Federal since zeroization is required for FIPS 140-2, Level 1.

_audit_fix: WRONG: The fact conflates two distinct SQLite compile-time macros: per the source OWASP C-Based Toolchain Hardening Cheat Sheet's own macro table, SQLITE_SECURE_DELETE

subject: SameSite cookie attribute default
truth: Modern major browsers (Chrome 80+/Feb 2020, Firefox 69+, Safari 13+) already default unspecified cookies to SameSite=Lax — don't assume the old None-by-default behavior. But don't rely on that implicit default for sensitive cookies either: legacy/unsupported clients silently fall back to no SameSite restriction (behaving like None), and Lax alone doesn't block all XS-Leaks vectors (e.g., top-level GET navigation can still carry the cookie). Explicitly set SameSite=Lax or Strict on sensitive cookies, and use SameSite=None only together with Secure when cross-site delivery is genuinely required.
quote: SameSite cookies are a strong **defense-in-depth** mechanism against **some** classes of XS Leaks and [CSRF attacks](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Preventio
source: OWASP CS: Cross-site leaks Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

subject: Ruby ActiveRecord delete with hash
truth: Retract or rewrite. The cited OWASP example `Project.delete(:name => 'name')` is NOT vulnerable — ActiveRecord parameterizes hash-based conditions internally, and OWASP lists it as a correct/safe usage alongside create!/where/update_attributes. There is no bad/good pairing in the source at that location. If a Ruby "delete" landmine fact is wanted, it should instead warn against string-interpolating user input into a query, e.g. bad: `Project.delete_all("name = '#{params[:name]}'")`, good: `Project.where(name: params[:name]).delete_all` (ActiveRecord) or `DB[:projects].where(name: params[:name]).delete` (Sequel) — a fix that actually corresponds to the DELETE operation shown.
quote: Project.delete(:name => 'name')
source: OWASP CS: Query Parameterization Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA
_audit_fix: WRONG: code_good is an unrelated INSERT statement using a prepared statement for different columns (name, age, gender) — it does not fix or even correspond to the DELE

subject: Unvalidated memory storage for agent memory
truth: Validate, sanitize, and scan for sensitive data and injection patterns before storing data in agent memory.

code_good:
def add(self, content: str, memory_type: str = "conversation"):
    # Validate content
    if len(content) > self.MAX_ITEM_LENGTH:
        content = content[:self.MAX_ITEM_LENGTH]
    
    # Scan for sensitive data patterns
    if self._contains_sensitive_data(content):
        content = self._redact_sensitive_data(content)
    
    # Scan for injection patterns
    content = self._sanitize_injection_attempts(content)
    
    # Create integrity-checked memory entry
    entry = {
        "content": content,
        "type": memory_type,
        "timestamp": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "user_id": self.user_id,
        "checksum": self._compute_checksum(content)
    }
    
    self.memories.append(entry)
    self._enforce_limits()
source: OWASP CS: AI Agent Security Cheat Sheet
license_note: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series, CC BY-SA

source: OWASP CS: DOM Clobbering Prevention Cheat Sheet

subject: SMS-based MFA
truth: Per NIST SP 800-63B-4 (finalized July 2025), SMS/PSTN-delivered OTP is a "restricted authenticator": it remains permitted, but only with documented risk mitigations (e.g., verifying the number is bound to a specific device, monitoring for SIM swap/number porting), explicit user notification of the risk, an updated risk assessment, and an active migration plan toward stronger methods. For new designs, prefer TOTP, push notifications, or WebAuthn/FIDO2 over SMS/PSTN OTP.
quote: > NIST SP 800-63B-4 designates SMS and PSTN-delivered codes as a *restricted* authenticator because of SS7 interception, SIM-swap, and number-porting attacks.
source: OWASP CS: Multifactor Authentication Cheat Sheet

_audit_fix: MISLEADING: NIST SP 800-63B-4 (finalized 2025) classifies SMS/PSTN OTP as a "restricted authenticator," meaning it remains permitted provided the organization offers altern

subject: Authentication error messages

IF USER_EXISTS(username) THEN
    password_hash=HASH(password)
    IS_VALID=LOOKUP_CREDENTIALS_IN_STORE(username, password_hash)
    IF NOT IS_VALID THEN
        RETURN Error("Invalid Username or Password!")
    ENDIF
ELSE
   RETURN Error("Invalid Username or Password!")
ENDIF
code_good:
password_hash=HASH(password)
IS_VALID=LOOKUP_CREDENTIALS_IN_STORE(username, password_hash)
IF NOT IS_VALID THEN
   RETURN Error("Invalid Username or Password!")
ENDIF
